title: Kitesurfing the Meltemi — Greek Islands Wind Guide
meta_title: Meltemi Wind Kitesurfing Greece 2026 | Conditions Guide
meta_description: Master the meltemi wind for kitesurfing in Greece. Learn conditions, gear, technique & safety. Find expert gear at Surf Store.
excerpt: The meltemi wind is Greece's legendary summer thermal, delivering consistent 15–25 knot conditions perfect for advanced kitesurfers.
body_html:
Understanding These Conditions

The meltemi wind is a seasonal northerly that dominates Greece and the Aegean Sea from June through September, driven by pressure differences between North Africa and mainland Europe. It arrives with remarkable consistency—often blowing 15–25 knots by midday, occasionally gusting past 30 knots on peak days. Unlike the chaotic thermal winds of inland Europe, the meltemi is steady, relatively predictable, and blessed with long daylight hours.

What makes meltemi wind kitesurfing unique is the combination of reliability and geography. The wind tends to strengthen as the day progresses, peaking in the afternoon. Mornings often start light or glassy; by 11 a.m. you'll feel pressure building, and by 2–4 p.m. you have solid, rideable conditions. The wind also channels through island passages and down coastal valleys, creating natural wind corridors where conditions are most consistent. Swell is typically small and choppy—this is thermal wind, not Atlantic storm energy—so water state is manageable even when air-side gusts are fierce.

Water temperature hovers around 20–24°C in mid-summer, cooling slightly as September approaches. Conditions can shift dramatically on rare non-meltemi days; expect flat water and unreliable wind if a low-pressure system moves through. But for week-long trips in July or August, the meltemi rarely disappoints.

Best Gear for These Conditions

Because meltemi conditions are steady and relatively strong, you'll need versatile kite sizes and boards built for consistent, powered riding.

Kites: Pack a 12m or 14m as your core setup. A 12m Duotone or Cabrinha will handle the 20–30 knot range comfortably; a 14m gives you margin on lighter mornings and early-week lulls. If you're a lighter rider or prefer tricks, a 10m is valuable insurance for the strongest days. Avoid oversized kites (17m+)—they're unnecessary in reliable meltemi and create fatigue in extended sessions.

Boards: A freeride or wave board in the 135–150cm range is ideal. Duotone, Cabrinha, Nobile, and JP Australia all stock boards suited to choppy, powered conditions with good directional control. The water is small and textured, not swell-driven, so you're not chasing waves—you're carving powered turns and keeping moving. A slightly smaller board (130–140cm) suits riders looking for tricks; a 145–155cm gives more float and stability if you're cruising and exploring the islands.

Wetsuits: A 3/2mm or 4/3mm from ION or NeilPryde is standard for early and mid-summer. By August, water temps creep up; a 2/2mm or shorty becomes an option. Avoid thick 5/4mm suits—you'll overheat in 25°C water during intense afternoon sessions. Many riders choose a 3/2mm and layer with a rash vest on days with strong UV exposure.

Technique Tips

The meltemi's consistency rewards rhythm and efficiency over aggression. Here's how to adapt:

  • Start early: Launch by 10 a.m. if possible. Morning wind is light but workable; you'll build fitness and technique in gentler conditions before the afternoon rush. Crowds also tend to thin by lunchtime.
  • Ride powered, not overpowered: The wind is reliable enough that you don't need to squeeze every gust. Keep your kite in a neutral, stable position. Let the steady pressure do the work instead of constant steering.
  • Use the channels: Island passages and coastal gaps channel the meltemi into narrow corridors. Local knowledge pays dividends—ask other riders or your accommodation host where the premium spots are on any given day.
  • Manage chop: Water is choppy but predictable. Keep your board angle slightly across the wind, use bent knees to absorb texture, and avoid hard edge work in confused sections. Smooth carves trump aggressive tricks in these conditions.
  • Respect the afternoon intensity: By 3–4 p.m., gusts can exceed 30 knots. If you're fatigued, swap to a smaller kite or head in. Fatigue in strong wind breeds mistakes.

Safety Checklist

The meltemi is reliable but not forgiving. Respect these risks:

  • Offshore wind: Many Greek islands have bays open to the north. The meltemi can channel straight offshore, pushing riders away from land. Always rig near a bay or beach sheltered from direct northern exposure. Know your exit strategy before you launch.
  • Strong afternoon gusts: Sudden 35+ knot surges are common. Use a smaller kite than you think you need, especially if you're solo or less experienced. A blown knee or shoulder injury in the Aegean is a serious problem.
  • Dehydration: Summer Greek heat + long sessions = rapid fluid loss. Drink before, during (if you can), and after. Pack electrolytes, not just water.
  • Sun exposure: UV intensity is ferocious. Reapply sunscreen every two hours, wear a rash vest, and consider a hat for breaks onshore. Sunburn and heat exhaustion degrade judgment quickly.
  • Local maritime traffic: Ferries, fishing vessels, and cargo ships use Aegean passages. Stay in designated kite zones and be aware of shipping lanes, especially near larger islands.
  • Shallow rocks and reefs: Many Greek launch spots have rocky shallows or submerged hazards. Scout your beach at low tide, wear reef shoes if needed, and ask locals about hazards.

Our Gear Recommendations at Surf Store

For summer meltemi trips, we recommend building a two-kite quiver and matching board setup. Stock a 12m and 14m kite from Duotone, Cabrinha, or NeilPryde—all three brands excel in reliable, predictable wind. Pair these with a freeride or wave board from Cabrinha, Duotone, Nobile, or JP Australia in the 135–150cm range; these sizes balance float, control, and manoeuvrability in choppy meltemi conditions. For wetsuits, our 3/2mm or 4/3mm suits from ION or NeilPryde are proven performers in 20–24°C water—stretchy, durable, and designed for active riding. Visit surf-store.com to browse our full range of Duotone, Cabrinha, NeilPryde, and Nobile gear, or contact our team for personalised advice on your Greece trip setup.

title: Kitesurfing the Mistral — South France Wind Guide 2026
meta_title: Mistral Wind Kitesurfing France 2026 | Surf Store
meta_description: Master the mistral wind for kitesurfing in France. Learn gear selection, technique tips, and safety. Find it at Surf Store.
excerpt: The mistral wind brings powerful, consistent conditions to Southern France. Learn how to harness this famous wind safely and effectively.
body_html:
Understanding These Conditions

The mistral wind is a cold, northwesterly jet that funnels down the Rhône Valley in southern France, creating some of Europe's most reliable and powerful wind windows. Riders heading to spots like Montpellier, the Camargue, and the Mediterranean coast live for the mistral's consistency—it can blow for days or even weeks during winter and spring, building anticipation and delivering unbeatable sessions.

What makes the mistral wind kitesurfing so distinctive is its intensity and reliability. This wind typically ranges from 15–35+ knots, with gusts regularly exceeding 40 knots in exposed areas. Because it's channelled through a valley, it's notably steady—you'll see far fewer lulls than with thermal or sea-breeze winds. The air is cold and often dry, which can affect water temperature significantly. The fetch is excellent, meaning wave conditions often accompany the wind, particularly on the Mediterranean side.

Mistral sessions are not forgiving for unprepared riders. The combination of strong, steady wind and frequent bigger swells demands respect, excellent equipment, and solid technique. Even experienced riders can find themselves overpowered if they misjudge the conditions or choose the wrong kite size.

Best Gear for These Conditions

For mistral wind riding, smaller kites are your friend. Most sessions call for a 12–15m kite when the wind is in the 20–30 knot range; drop to 9–12m when gusts push toward 35+ knots. In lighter mistral days (15–18 knots), a 17m may work, but be prepared to downsize quickly. Brands like Duotone, Cabrinha, and NeilPryde all excel in these conditions—their freestyle and wave-specific kites are built to handle the strong, gusty nature of valley winds.

For your board, the mistral wind often comes with chunky swell, so a wave board in the 135–155cm range is ideal. Freestyle boards in the 135–150cm range also work well if you're planning tricks rather than waves. Nobile, Fanatic, and JP Australia all offer quality options here. Boards need good pop and edge hold; this wind demands precision.

Wetsuit thickness is crucial—mistral air is cold, sometimes below 10°C in winter. A 4/3mm or 5/4mm wetsuit from ION or NeilPryde will protect you during extended sessions. Some riders even go for a 6/5mm or hood combo if the forecast shows extended duration. The Mediterranean water itself can be quite cold in winter, so don't underestimate the chill factor when wet.

Technique Tips

The mistral wind's steady nature means you can commit to technique without worrying about sudden lulls—a real advantage for progression. However, its gustiness demands active bar control. Keep your depower range ready at all times; don't fly your kite fully extended in the window. Edge hard and maintain good body position; the wind rewards precise, controlled movements.

When riding in waves, use the mistral's consistency to your advantage. Plan your lines ahead of time and commit fully—hesitation is punished. The wind's strength means you can work smaller waves effectively, so scout your break and pick your entry carefully.

In freestyle, the steady pressure allows longer tricks. The challenge is managing overpowering gusts, so err on the side of a smaller kite than you think you need. A properly sized kite for mistral wind gives you room to breathe and recover if you stack.

Safety Checklist

  • Kite sizing: Downsize aggressively. If you're unsure, go smaller—underhooked beats overpowered every time.
  • Launch zone: The mistral can be fierce onshore or side-onshore. Always launch from a clear, flat beach and check for obstacles downwind before you go on the water.
  • Wear a helmet: Gusty wind increases impact risk. A quality helmet is non-negotiable in mistral conditions.
  • Check your equipment: Inspect your kite, lines, and board before every session. High wind puts extra strain on gear.
  • Tell someone your plan: Mistral sessions can last hours. Let a friend know where you're going and when you expect to return.
  • Know your exit: Identify a safe beach or recovery zone before you launch, especially if you're riding an unfamiliar spot.
  • Watch the thermometer: Cold water + cold air = rapid heat loss. Take breaks, warm up, and don't stay out longer than your body can handle.

Our Gear Recommendations at Surf Store

For mistral wind kitesurfing, we stock everything you need. We recommend a 12–15m kite from Duotone or Cabrinha for typical mistral sessions; both brands handle valley wind exceptionally well. Pair this with a wave or freestyle board in the 135–155cm range from Nobile, Fanatic, or JP Australia—these deliver the control and responsiveness that mistral conditions demand.

For protection, choose a 4/3mm or 5/4mm wetsuit from ION or NeilPryde depending on water temperature and session duration. ION's gear is particularly popular among mistral riders for its durability and warmth.

Browse our full range at surf-store.com and get in touch with our team—we have real on-water experience in European wind and can advise on the exact setup for your level and local conditions.

title: Kitesurfing the Tramontane — Roussillon Wind Guide
meta_title: Tramontane Wind Kitesurfing France 2026 Guide
meta_description: Master the tramontane wind for kitesurfing in France. Learn conditions, gear choices, technique, and safety. Shop expert equipment at Surf Store.
excerpt: The tramontane is France's most powerful and consistent wind for kitesurfing, blowing cold and hard down the Rhône valley and across the Mediterranean coast.
body_html:
Understanding These Conditions

The tramontane wind is a powerful, dry northwesterly gust that dominates the Languedoc and Provence regions of southern France, particularly around the Côte d'Azur and near Montpellier. It typically occurs in winter and spring, arriving suddenly and building to sustained 20–35 knots, with gusts reaching 40+ knots in peak events. Unlike the gentle sea breezes of summer, the tramontane is cold, unforgiving, and often accompanied by low, turbulent cloud cover.

This wind is born when cold Atlantic air masses collide with high pressure systems over the Mediterranean. The Mistral's close cousin, the tramontane wind kitesurfing community in France treats it with respect. It funnels down river valleys and across plains, creating a wall of wind that can appear almost without warning. Conditions are typically gusty and variable in direction, making it both thrilling and demanding. Spots like Gruissan and Canet-en-Roussillon see regular tramontane activity, attracting experienced riders from across Europe.

Water temperatures in these conditions drop to 8–12°C, and the wind often carries spray and salt-laden gusts. The sky may darken dramatically, and visibility can reduce quickly. The tramontane wind kitesurfing enthusiasts know to check local forecasts obsessively—this wind can switch on and off in hours, and its intensity makes it unsuitable for beginners.

Best Gear for These Conditions

For tramontane wind kitesurfing, you need equipment that handles power, gustiness, and rapid transitions. Here's what works:

  • Kite size: In 25–35 knot tramontane conditions, a 12m or 13m kite is your go-to. If you're caught in lighter lulls within a gusty session, a 14m or 15m kite from Duotone or Cabrinha gives you options without overcomplicating your quiver. For edge cases, some riders carry both a 12m and a 15m to match rapidly shifting conditions.
  • Board type and size: A directional or freestyle board in the 135–155cm range is ideal. The tramontane's power and gustiness demand a board with good control; a Nobile, Fanatic, or JP Australia freestyle board in this size range will keep you stable through wind swings. Avoid twin-tips if you're new to strong wind—a directional gives you more leverage against surprise gusts.
  • Wetsuit: Water temperature is cold—go thick. A 4/3mm or even 5/4mm wetsuit from ION or NeilPryde is essential. The wind chill amplifies heat loss, so invest in a quality chest-zip or back-zip suit with good seals. Gloves and booties are not luxury; they're survival gear.
  • Bar and safety: Use a quality five-line or seven-line bar with a reliable safety release system. The gustiness means accidental depower moments happen; your bar should respond instantly.

Technique Tips

Tramontane wind kitesurfing demands technique adjustments because gusts and lulls arrive without warning:

  • Keep your kite higher in the window: Fly your kite toward 11–1 o'clock most of the time. This gives you more leverage to recover if a gust hits and you get pressed downwind. It also lets you edge hard without risking a full power-on scenario.
  • Anticipate lulls: The tramontane's nature means strong wind followed by relative calm. When you feel pressure drop, immediately sheet in and prepare to pop and ride. Don't wait for consistent tension; stay active.
  • Shorten your lines or adjust your bar position: In extreme gusts, moving your hands inboard on the bar or using slightly shorter lines (20m instead of 24m) gives you tighter control and faster response time.
  • Lean back in your harness: The tramontane's intensity demands solid harness work. Sit deep, keep your centre of gravity low, and use your legs to absorb sudden power surges.
  • Ride longer edges: Don't fight the wind—use it. Build edge pressure methodically, then release gradually. Sudden moves invite backrolls or wipeouts in gusty conditions.

Safety Checklist

Tramontane wind kitesurfing carries specific risks:

  • Sudden gust events: The wind can jump 10+ knots in seconds. Always check your forecasts before launching, and have a bailout plan. If wind exceeds your comfort zone, don't go on the water.
  • Cold water shock: Water temperatures around 8–12°C cause rapid heat loss and potential hypothermia. Never underestimate thick wetsuits and limit session time in extreme cold.
  • Spray and visibility: Salt spray reduces visibility and makes it hard to see landmarks or other riders. Wear a helmet; impacts at speed in cold, choppy water are serious.
  • Rip currents and offshore wind: Some tramontane spots, especially near river mouths, funnel water seaward. Confirm you can get back to shore before launching.
  • Kite tangling and line snarl: Gusty winds and turbulence cause kites to collapse and recover chaotically. Practise your launch and landing in controlled settings first. Use a spotter on shore whenever possible.
  • Equipment fatigue: The power in tramontane wind stresses your bar, lines, and kite. Inspect gear after every session and replace anything showing wear.

Our Gear Recommendations at Surf Store

At Surf Store, we've rigged countless riders for tramontane conditions across the French Mediterranean. For this wind, we recommend a Duotone, Cabrinha, or Point7 kite in the 12–15m range, paired with a Nobile, Fanatic, or JP Australia freestyle or directional board in the 135–155cm size. Layer yourself in a 4/3mm or 5/4mm ION or NeilPryde wetsuit, and don't skip gloves or booties. We stock all these brands and can help you match your weight, skill level, and local conditions to the right setup. Contact us or visit surf-store.com to browse our full range and get expert advice before you launch into tramontane territory.

title: Kitesurfing the Levante — Tarifa East Wind Guide
meta_title: Levante Wind Tarifa Kitesurfing Guide 2026
meta_description: Master the Levante at Tarifa with expert tips on wind patterns, gear selection, and technique. Shop kites and wetsuits at Surf Store.
excerpt: The Levante wind at Tarifa brings powerful, consistent easterly conditions beloved by kitesurfers seeking reliable performance and skill progression.
body_html:
Understanding These Conditions

The Levante wind at Tarifa is one of Europe's most consistent and powerful thermal winds. This warm, southeasterly wind blows from the Mediterranean across the Strait of Gibraltar, driven by temperature differences between the Atlantic and the warmed Mediterranean basin. During spring and summer, the Levante can gust into the 20–30+ knot range, especially in the afternoon when heating is strongest.

What makes the Levante wind at Tarifa kitesurfing so distinctive is its reliability. It typically kicks in mid-morning, builds steadily through midday, and often peaks in the late afternoon. Many riders head out after 2 p.m. when the wind has fully developed. The wind is usually steady and thermals-driven rather than gusty, though afternoon sea-breeze fronts can introduce chop and variability. Because thermal winds heat the water column, conditions often become choppy by mid-afternoon, especially on busy beach days.

Tarifa's bay geography—sheltered on the Atlantic side but exposed to the Mediterranean—means the Levante wind at Tarifa channels between the African coast and Spain, funnelling extra power through the Strait. Expect a mostly side-onshore direction and consistent pressure once it locks in.

Best Gear for These Conditions

The Levante's power demands careful kite and board sizing.

Kite size: On a solid 20+ knot day, most intermediate to advanced riders will use a 12m or 14m. Beginners should bring a 17m as backup, though you'll likely spend most of your session on a 14m. If the Levante is particularly strong (25+ knots), drop to a 12m or even 9m. Look to brands like Duotone, Cabrinha, and NeilPryde for stable, predictable performance in powered conditions—these are proven choices at Tarifa.

Board type and size: The choppy water demands a responsive board with volume to handle the chop without bogging down. A freestyle or all-round board in the 135–155cm range gives you forgiveness and pop. Cabrinha, Nobile, and Fanatic boards in this range are excellent choices. Heavier riders may prefer 145–155cm; lighter riders 135–145cm. Avoid tiny wave boards—you need float to survive the afternoon chop.

Wetsuit: The Mediterranean warms in summer, but spring and autumn Levante sessions can be cool. A 3/2mm wetsuit covers most of the year; switch to 4/3mm if you're going out in May or late September. ION and NeilPryde are reliable, stretchy options that won't restrict your movement in powered conditions.

Technique Tips

The Levante's steady, thermal nature is forgiving compared to gusty Atlantic wind, but the building chop demands smart technique.

  • Timing: Arrive early, get on a bigger kite, and warm up on smaller manoeuvres before the wind peaks and chop builds. By 3 p.m., the bay often becomes rough; experienced riders prefer to launch by 1 p.m.
  • Kite control: Thermal winds are usually smooth, so aggressive edge pressure works well. Keep your kite lower in the window when the gusts come and edge hard into chop to maintain speed.
  • Stance and balance: Choppy water forgives sloppy pop but rewards a centred stance. Keep your weight neutral over the board and let the kite do the work rather than forcing tricks.
  • Downwind drift: The Levante pushes you toward Africa. Plan your upwind rides early; don't get drifted too far out before the afternoon gusts arrive.

Safety Checklist

  • Afternoon overheating: The Levante thermal builds relentlessly. Even experienced riders can underestimate how strong it gets. Rig conservatively and plan to land before chop makes recovery difficult.
  • Offshore component: Although side-onshore, the Levante can have a slight offshore push. Always launch with a buddy, signal the beach, and carry a whistle if you're pushed beyond the break.
  • Chop handling: The afternoon chop is steep and short-period. Avoid tricks that require a clean landing in choppy water; stick to transitions and edge manoeuvres until you're confident in broken conditions.
  • Sunburn and dehydration: Thermal wind days are hot. Wear SPF 50+ sunscreen, drink water before you launch, and take breaks to rehydrate. The heat builds steadily and can sneak up on you.
  • Kite security: Always use a leash and backup system. The steady wind is reliable, but afternoon thermals can spike suddenly. Never leave your kite unattended on the beach.

Our Gear Recommendations at Surf Store

For Levante wind at Tarifa kitesurfing, we recommend a quiver approach at Surf Store. Start with a Duotone or Cabrinha 14m kite—both brands are proven at Tarifa and deliver smooth, predictable power. Pair it with a Cabrinha, Nobile, or Fanatic freestyle board in the 140–150cm range; this size balances float and response for choppy thermal conditions.

For wetsuits, stock a 3/2mm from ION or NeilPryde for late spring and summer; add a 4/3mm for cooler months. If you're serious about Tarifa sessions, consider a Duotone or Cabrinha 12m as your second kite—it'll handle the strongest afternoon gusts and extend your session window.

Our team at Surf Store has real experience in Tarifa and the Mediterranean. We can help you dial in your setup and ensure your gear matches the Levante's power and chop. Visit us online or get in touch for a consultation before your next trip.

title: Kitesurfing the Poniente — Tarifa West Wind Guide
meta_title: Poniente Wind Tarifa Kitesurfing Guide 2026
meta_description: Master the Poniente wind at Tarifa with our expert kitesurfing guide. Learn conditions, gear, and technique tips. Shop now at Surf Store.
excerpt: The Poniente wind brings consistent westerly conditions to Tarifa—here's how to read it, gear for it, and ride it safely.
body_html:
Understanding These Conditions

The Poniente wind at Tarifa is one of Europe's most celebrated and reliable wind phenomena. This westerly wind funnels through the Strait of Gibraltar and hits the beaches around Tarifa with consistent, predictable strength—typically in the 12–25-knot range, though it can gust considerably higher. The Poniente wind Tarifa kitesurfing setup is the dream scenario for most water sports enthusiasts: steady, well-organised swell, good visibility, and enough thermal energy to keep it blowing from spring through early autumn.

What makes the Poniente special is its reliability and character. Unlike random Atlantic swells or localised thermals, this wind is driven by pressure systems across the Atlantic and the African coast. It typically builds through the morning, peaks in the afternoon, and eases as the sun sets. The water is relatively warm (17–22°C depending on season), and the wind corridor is wide enough to accommodate everything from beginners to advanced freestyle riders. However, the Poniente can be deceptive: what looks mellow on the beach can develop into a punchy, choppy ride once you're on the water, especially on swell days when the Atlantic is active.

Understanding the tidal window is crucial too. The strait's tidal flow affects water movement and can create wind shadowing near cliffs or rocky outcrops, so know your beach zone and avoid areas where the wind dies behind topography.

Best Gear for These Conditions

For reliable Poniente wind Tarifa kitesurfing sessions, you need versatile kit that handles both moderate and gusty conditions. Most riders working the 12–20-knot steady window will want a 13–15m all-rounder or wave kite from brands like Duotone or Cabrinha. These sizes let you ride comfortably in the median wind, yet stay under control if the Poniente spikes to 22–25 knots. If you're a lighter rider or prefer freestyle, a 12–13m directional or freestyle kite gives you more response; heavier riders or those chasing waves should look at a 15–16m in the lighter end of the forecast.

Board choice matters enormously here. For freestyle and light-wind sessions, a freestyle board in the 135–150cm range from brands like Nobile or Fanatic lets you land tricks and ride flat water with ease. For wave-riding and chop, a directional wave board in the 140–160cm range from JP Australia or Tabou gives you drive and hold in rough water. Many Tarifa regulars carry two boards: a smaller freestyle for calm mornings and a larger, stiffer wave board for afternoon gusts and swell.

Wetsuit choice is straightforward but important. Spring and summer (April–September) favour a 3/2mm or short-arm rashguard from ION or NeilPryde. Autumn (October–November) and winter sessions demand a 4/3mm or 5/4mm full suit—the Poniente keeps blowing even when the water cools. Always rinse in fresh water after sessions; salt and UV damage gear quickly.

Technique Tips

Riding the Poniente effectively means adapting to variable wind intensity and chop. First, master your wind window awareness: the Poniente often builds in gusts, so position yourself slightly downwind of your intended riding line so you have room to drift back if the wind jumps. This prevents being driven into shallow water or the beach.

When the Poniente is light (12–15 knots), use smooth, wide kite movements and keep your board flat on the water. Avoid jerky inputs; let the wind do the work. If you're doing freestyle, pop your tricks early in your gust and recover downwind.

In gusts (18–25 knots), reduce your kite's power by moving it toward the edge of your wind window and keeping it moving. Keep your bar near your body to kill excess power quickly, and use your legs—bend your knees to absorb chop impacts and stay loose. Lean back slightly more than you would in light wind, and trust your edge control.

The chop at Tarifa—especially afternoon chop when swell meets the Poniente—requires a composed, athletic riding style. Look ahead, anticipate bumps, and adjust your weight forward or back a fraction before you hit them. Avoid rigid, tense arms; let your shoulders roll with the board.

Safety Checklist

  • Check the forecast daily: The Poniente can shift direction or weaken suddenly if Atlantic systems move. Always confirm wind direction and speed before heading out.
  • Wear a helmet and impact vest: Chop and shallow water increase impact risk. A certified helmet and 5mm impact vest are non-negotiable at Tarifa.
  • Watch for tidal currents: The strait's tidal flow is strong. Check tide tables and avoid riding if currents are sweeping sideways—you can drift far downwind unexpectedly.
  • Stay clear of rocks and cliffs: Wind shadowing and back-eddies near headlands can collapse your kite. Stick to the main beach launch zones.
  • Buddy system: Always ride with a friend or in a group. If something goes wrong—a kite malfunction, injury, or fatigue—you need eyes and hands nearby.
  • Know your exit strategy: Identify where to pack down before you launch. Returning to shore in the Poniente can be demanding, so scout a safe landing zone.
  • Check your gear: Bar systems and bridle lines take a beating in choppy wind. Inspect for fraying, loose knots, and kite fabric before each session.

Our Gear Recommendations at Surf Store

At Surf Store, we've equipped hundreds of Tarifa regulars with the right kit. For the Poniente, we stock Duotone and Cabrinha all-rounder kites in the 12–16m range—both brands excel in variable, gusty wind and hold their shape beautifully across a wide wind envelope. If you prefer wave-riding, ask us about Cabrinha's wave-specific models in the 12–14m bracket; they carve and respond instantly in chop.

For boards, we recommend Nobile or Fanatic freestyle boards in the 135–150cm range if you love tricks and light conditions, or JP Australia and Tabou wave boards in the 140–160cm range if you want drive and control in the afternoon chaos. Many of our customers own both—it's worth the investment if you're spending weeks at Tarifa.

Wetsuits: ION and NeilPryde 3/2mm suits are perfect for spring and summer; move to 4/3mm or 5/4mm by October. We also stock ION impact vests and helmets—essential safety kit that too many riders overlook.

Visit surf-store.com to browse our full Duotone, Cabrinha, Nobile, JP Australia, and ION range, or reach out to our team. We ship across Europe and our staff ride these conditions regularly—we'll help you dial in your quiver.

title: Kitesurfing the Bora — Croatia Adriatic Wind Guide
meta_title: Bora Wind Croatia Kitesurfing Guide 2026
meta_description: Master the Bora wind in Croatia with expert kitesurfing and windsurfing tips, gear recommendations, and safety advice. Shop now at Surf Store.
excerpt: The Bora wind transforms Croatia's coast into a world-class kitesurfing and windsurfing destination—learn conditions, gear, technique, and safety.
body_html:
Understanding These Conditions

The Bora wind in Croatia is one of Europe's most distinctive and powerful natural phenomena. This cold, dry northeasterly wind funnels down from the Dinaric Mountains onto the Adriatic coast, particularly affecting the Dalmatian region. When it arrives, it can sustain speeds of 25–50 knots, with gusts pushing beyond 60 knots on extreme days.

What makes the Bora wind in Croatia unique is its predictability and strength. It typically flows strongest during winter (November to March) and spring, but can occur year-round. The wind is characterised by sharp, gusty conditions rather than smooth sustained power—think powerful but turbulent. The sea state varies: in the bay areas and lee zones, water remains choppy but manageable; on exposed coasts, fetch builds quickly, creating steep, short-period waves ideal for wave riding.

Temperature is another critical factor. Bora conditions bring cold air, so water temperatures drop to 8–12°C in winter. Visibility is usually excellent—clear skies and low humidity mean you'll have stunning views and reliable forecasts. This makes the Bora wind in Croatia a premium choice for serious riders who understand risk management.

Best Gear for These Conditions

The Bora demands respect in your equipment choices.

Kite and sail sizing: For kitesurfing, high wind means smaller is better. A 12m or 13m freestyle or wave kite from Duotone or Cabrinha works well for Bora days above 25 knots; drop to 9m or 10m when gusts exceed 40 knots. For windsurfers, you'll want 4.5m to 5.5m freewave or wave sails from NeilPryde or Gaastra—avoid anything larger unless you're in sheltered bays on lighter Bora days.

Board selection: Choose a freestyle or wave board in the 135–150cm range for kitesurfing; these sizes offer control and manoeuvrability in chaotic conditions. For windsurfing, a 75–95 litre wave or freewave board from JP Australia or Fanatic is ideal. The goal is low volume and responsiveness—big boards catch too much wind and become uncontrollable.

Wetsuits: Water temperatures are cold. A 4/3mm or 5/4mm winter wetsuit from ION or NeilPryde is non-negotiable; on extreme Bora days, many locals layer a 3/2mm underneath for extra insulation. Don't skimp here—hypothermia sets in quickly in the Adriatic winter.

Technique Tips

Bora conditions demand aggressive edge control and mental preparation.

  • Board positioning: Keep your weight forward and feet wide. The gusty, turbulent nature of the Bora wind in Croatia means sudden power surges; this stance absorbs shocks and prevents wash-outs.
  • Kite/sail pressure: Expect dramatic power fluctuations. Keep tension on your bar or boom at all times. For kitesurfers, riding with a lower sheeted kite and using quick, precise movements helps manage gusts. Windsurfers should remain slightly over-sheeted to stay powered through the lulls.
  • Wave riding: If waves form, use them to your advantage. The Bora often creates shorter, steeper waves—attack them aggressively. Keep your approach downwind and commit to turns early.
  • Navigation: Always ride in areas with natural lee or bay protection on your first Bora session. Exposed coast can mean being pushed far offshore fast. Stick to marked zones or ride with locals who know the bathymetry.

Safety Checklist

Wind variability: Bora gusts are sudden and severe. Always carry a smaller kite or sail than you think you'll need. If you're between sizes, go smaller.

Cold water and air: Hypothermia is real. Wear appropriate wetsuits, avoid paddling in cold water, and set time limits on sessions. Bring a warm towel and change of clothes to shore.

Offshore risk: The Bora blows offshore on exposed coasts. If you lose your equipment, you drift toward open sea. Always wear a leash, ride with a buddy, and inform someone of your location and expected return time.

Equipment durability: Bora conditions put strain on gear. Inspect your kite bridle, boom, and lines before launching. Use new or recently serviced equipment; worn gear fails under sustained gust loads.

Weather windows: Check local forecasts obsessively. A 10-knot swing can mean the difference between manageable and dangerous. Launch early before afternoon gusts intensify, and exit when conditions spike.

Our Gear Recommendations at Surf Store

At Surf Store, we specialise in equipping serious riders for extreme European conditions—and the Bora wind in Croatia is exactly what we prepare for.

For kitesurfing, we stock Duotone and Cabrinha freestyle and wave kites in the 9m, 10m, 12m, and 13m range—sizes that let you dial in precisely for Bora power. Pair these with freestyle or wave boards from Duotone, Fanatic, or Nobile in the 135–150cm range. These boards are built for edge hold and response in gusty air.

For windsurfing, NeilPryde and Gaastra freewave or wave sails in the 4.5m to 5.5m range paired with JP Australia or Fanatic wave boards (75–95 litres) give you the control and durability Bora demands.

Everyone riding needs a 4/3mm or 5/4mm winter wetsuit from ION or NeilPryde. We also stock heavy-duty leashes, harnesses, and spare bridles so you can stay safe and mobile.

Visit surf-store.com or contact our team directly. We're water sports instructors ourselves—we've ridden the Bora and can help you choose gear that matches your skill level and the specific bay or coast you're targeting. Ship across Europe in EUR.

Final Thoughts

The Bora wind in Croatia rewards preparation, respect, and quality gear. It's not beginner territory, but for experienced riders seeking raw, unfiltered conditions and stunning Adriatic scenery, few European winds compare.

title: The Sirocco Wind — Mediterranean Kitesurfing Guide
meta_title: Sirocco Wind Mediterranean Kitesurfing Guide 2026
meta_description: Master sirocco wind conditions for Mediterranean kitesurfing. Learn gear selection, technique, and safety. Shop now at Surf Store.
excerpt: The sirocco wind brings warm, gusty conditions to Mediterranean waters—here's how to harness it safely and effectively.
body_html:
Understanding These Conditions

The sirocco wind is a distinctive Mediterranean phenomenon that every kitesurfer in southern Europe should understand. This warm, often dusty wind sweeps northward from the Sahara across North Africa and into the Mediterranean basin, bringing unpredictable energy and challenging conditions. When the sirocco wind arrives, you'll experience sudden gusts, thermal turbulence, and rapid pressure changes that demand respect and preparation.

Sirocco conditions typically arrive in spring and autumn, characterised by warm air temperatures, variable wind speeds (often 15–30 knots with violent gusts), and a hazy, dusty atmosphere. The wind's direction is usually from the south or southeast, but the sirocco wind can shift suddenly, especially near coastal headlands and bays. Visibility may reduce due to Saharan dust, and the water often becomes choppy with short, steep waves.

What makes the sirocco wind particularly tricky is its thermal nature. Unlike consistent trade winds, this air mass creates unstable conditions where wind pressure fluctuates rapidly. You may experience dead calm periods followed by hard gusts that catch you off-guard. This unpredictability is why local knowledge and conservative gear choices are essential when the sirocco wind is forecast.

Best Gear for These Conditions

When riding in sirocco wind conditions, downsizing your kite is your best friend. The sudden gusts mean that a kite you'd normally use in steady wind becomes potentially dangerous. We recommend dropping one or even two sizes smaller than you would in consistent conditions. For most riders, this means going with a 12–14m kite from Duotone or Cabrinha rather than your usual 15–17m setup. If you're a lighter rider or experienced in gusty conditions, a 10–12m from NeilPryde or Cabrinha offers greater control and safety margins.

Board choice matters just as much as kite size. The sirocco wind's choppy water and unpredictable gusts favour smaller, more responsive boards. Choose a freestyle or wave board in the 130–145cm range from brands like Fanatic, JP Australia, or Nobile. Avoid large directional boards; you need something nimble that responds instantly to weight shifts and kite movements. A freestyle board gives you manoeuvrability to handle unexpected wind shifts without being thrown around.

Wetsuits should be 3/2mm or 4/3mm depending on season. The sirocco wind brings warm air, but Mediterranean water in spring and autumn remains cool. ION and NeilPryde both offer excellent suits in these thicknesses that balance warmth with mobility—crucial when you're constantly adjusting to wind swings. The dusty atmosphere also means investing in good quality eyewear to protect against sand and salt spray.

Technique Tips

Riding the sirocco wind successfully requires a defensive, conservative mindset. Keep your kite lower and more active than usual—constant small adjustments at 8 o'clock and 4 o'clock will help you manage gusts before they build dangerous power. Avoid aggressive edging or freestyle tricks when major gusts are forecast; save technical manoeuvres for the calm windows.

Stay alert to wind direction changes. The sirocco wind can shift 20–30 degrees within seconds near coastlines. Position yourself in open water away from headlands where wind shear is worst. When a strong gust hits, immediately sheet out (move your bar away) and steer downwind rather than fighting it. Your goal is to dissipate energy, not absorb it.

Use the calm spells between gusts to position yourself safely. If conditions deteriorate, don't hesitate to work your way back to shore. A 10-minute ride in manageable conditions beats a 30-minute struggle against the sirocco wind's mood swings. Build experience gradually; start on light-wind days when the sirocco wind is forecast but not yet intense.

Safety Checklist

  • Pre-flight inspection: Check your entire kite system before launching. Gusts can exploit weak stitching or small tears. Test your safety release on land.
  • Downsize aggressively: If you're unsure, go smaller. The sirocco wind's unpredictability rewards caution.
  • Launch and land in open beach: Avoid rocky coves and narrow beaches where side gusts concentrate and terrain turbulence increases.
  • Buddy system: Never ride alone. Have a friend or fellow rider watching from shore, especially on stronger sirocco wind days.
  • Know your exit strategy: Identify a clear route back to shore before launching. Dust and reduced visibility can disorient you quickly.
  • Wear a helmet and impact vest: Unpredictable conditions mean higher fall risk. Protect yourself.
  • Monitor weather updates: Sirocco wind forecasts change rapidly. Check conditions hourly and be ready to quit.
  • Avoid jumping and tricks: Save technical riding for stable wind days. Gusts mid-trick are dangerous.

Our Gear Recommendations at Surf Store

For sirocco wind conditions, we stock a carefully curated selection at Surf Store. We recommend Duotone or Cabrinha kites in the 12–14m range as your primary choice—both brands excel in gusty, unpredictable wind. For lighter riders or those seeking maximum control, NeilPryde kites at 10–12m offer excellent stability.

Pair your kite with a freestyle or wave board from Fanatic, JP Australia, or Nobile in the 130–145cm range. These boards respond instantly to your input and won't overpower you in sudden wind swings. Avoid full-on wave or directional boards; stick to versatile, responsive shapes.

For wetsuits, ION and NeilPryde both offer superb 3/2mm and 4/3mm suits that keep you warm without sacrificing mobility. Our team at Surf Store can help you choose the right thickness based on your riding season and local water temperature.

Visit surf-store.com to browse our full range of kites, boards, and wetsuits suited to Mediterranean conditions. Our staff have real on-water experience with the sirocco wind and can guide you toward a setup that keeps you safe, comfortable, and progressing.

title: Trade Wind Kitesurfing — Why It Is So Good
meta_title: Trade Winds Kitesurfing Canary Islands 2026 | Fuerteventu...
meta_description: Master trade winds kitesurfing in Fuerteventura. Learn conditions, gear sizes, technique tips & safety. Shop kites & boards at Surf Store.
excerpt: Trade winds deliver reliable, consistent Atlantic conditions for kitesurfing in Fuerteventura. Learn how to read them, choose the right gear, and ride safely.
body_html:
Understanding Trade Winds Kitesurfing Conditions

Trade winds are the reliable, steady wind systems that blow across subtropical latitudes—and the Canary Islands, particularly Fuerteventura, sit in the sweet spot. These winds typically arrive from the northeast at 12–20 knots, with peaks reaching 25 knots on stronger days. What makes trade winds ideal for trade winds kitesurfing is their consistency: you get predictable wind strength, minimal lulls, and stable direction, making them perfect for both progression and freestyle.

The Canary Islands benefit from the trade wind belt almost year-round, but peak season runs from spring through autumn. Fuerteventura's exposed beaches—particularly spots like Pozo Izquierdo and Flag Beach—funnel these winds and often see stronger gusts due to the island's geography. Unlike thermal or sea-breeze winds, trade winds maintain their strength throughout the day, giving you long, uninterrupted sessions. Water temperature hovers around 16–22°C depending on season, so a wetsuit is essential.

Understanding the consistency of trade winds kitesurfing in Fuerteventura is key: you're not chasing conditions—you're managing them. The challenge isn't finding wind; it's choosing the right gear so you can ride comfortably without being overpowered.

Best Gear for These Conditions

For trade wind sessions, your kite and board choice depends on your weight and skill level, but consistency is your advantage.

Kites

A 14–17m all-round freeride or freestyle kite handles the 12–20 knot window well. We recommend Duotone or Cabrinha models in the 15m size as a versatile middle ground—they're forgiving in light thermals and stable in stronger gusts. Lighter riders (under 70kg) may prefer 13–14m; heavier riders (over 85kg) should consider 16–17m. Avoid ultra-light wave kites in trade winds; you need stability and drive more than low-end pop.

Boards

A freestyle or freeride board in the 135–150cm range suits the steady power of trade winds. Duotone, Cabrinha, and Nobile boards in this size offer excellent edge hold and response in consistent pressure. If you're progressing tricks, a dedicated freestyle board gives you better pop and control. For pure freeride and cruising, a freeride-freestyle hybrid gives versatility across the wind window.

Wetsuits

Water temperatures in the Canary Islands call for a 3/2mm or 4/3mm spring suit or full suit depending on season. ION and NeilPryde wetsuits offer excellent warmth and flexibility for longer sessions in trade wind conditions. Winter months (November–February) may require a thicker 5/3mm or even a hooded suit.

Technique Tips

Trade winds reward smooth, efficient technique because the wind is steady—no need to hunt for pockets or recover from sudden lulls.

  • Ride with loose bar tension: Consistent wind means you can relax your grip. Keep the bar at chest height and let the kite do the work rather than muscling it around.
  • Edge hard early: On your heel edge, load the board immediately after take-off. Trade winds give you clean power delivery, so commit to edge early for better pop and smoother landings.
  • Stay centred: Consistency allows you to dial in your stance. Position your weight over the centre of the board for better control when you're learning tricks or adjusting direction.
  • Use the window efficiently: With steady wind, you can spend more time riding upwind and less time fighting drift. This frees mental energy for technique refinement.
  • Avoid over-sheeting in gusts: When wind spikes to 24–25 knots, don't depower aggressively; instead, ease the bar forward and ride the gust rather than fighting it.

Safety Checklist

Even predictable trade winds demand respect. Here's what to watch:

  • Gust management: Trade winds can spike 3–5 knots above average without warning. Always check the forecast and sail a kite one size smaller than your absolute limit. If gusts consistently exceed 25 knots, move to a lighter kite or smaller board.
  • Launch and landing zones: Fuerteventura's beaches are often crowded. Scout your spot, ensure clear sky above you, and never launch or land near swimmers, spectators, or other athletes.
  • Equipment inspection: Consistent, strong wind puts steady load on bridles and bladders. Check your kite before every session—look for tears, leaks, and bridle wear. A failing kite in trade winds can drop suddenly.
  • Buddy system: Always ride with a friend or in a monitored spot. If you're injured or exhausted, someone needs to see and help you.
  • Water temperature: Even if the air feels warm, water in the Canaries is cool. Wear adequate protection; hypothermia can sneak up during long sessions.
  • Riptides and currents: Popular spots like Pozo Izquierdo have strong currents. Know the local geography, ask local riders, and never fight a current—sail parallel to shore to escape it.

Our Gear Recommendations at Surf Store

We stock everything you need for consistent trade wind sessions across Fuerteventura and the Canary Islands. Our team rides these conditions regularly, so we know what works.

Kites: We carry Duotone and Cabrinha all-round freeride and freestyle kites in 14–17m sizes. For trade wind stability, we recommend Duotone or Cabrinha 15m as the go-to size for riders 70–85kg in medium wind.

Boards: We stock Duotone, Cabrinha, and Nobile freestyle and freeride boards in the 135–150cm range—all proven in consistent Atlantic conditions. Choose a freestyle board if you're focused on tricks; choose a freeride-freestyle hybrid if you want all-purpose versatility.

Wetsuits: ION and NeilPryde 3/2mm and 4/3mm spring suits and full suits keep you warm and mobile during long sessions. For winter, we stock 5/3mm and hooded options from both brands.

Visit surf-store.com to browse our full range, check current stock, and speak with our team about your specific weight, skill level, and local spot. We ship across Europe and offer expert advice on gear setup for trade wind riding.

title: Sea Breeze & Thermal Wind — Kitesurfing Guide
meta_title: Thermal Wind Sea Breeze Kitesurfing Guide 2026
meta_description: Master thermal sea breeze conditions for kitesurfing. Learn wind patterns, ideal gear, and technique tips. Shop expert gear at Surf Store.
excerpt: Thermal sea breeze creates predictable afternoon wind for kitesurfing—perfect for progression and light-wind sessions when you know how to read it.
body_html:
Understanding These Conditions

A thermal sea breeze is one of the most reliable wind patterns for kitesurfing in temperate coastal regions across Europe. It forms when land heats up faster than the sea during the day. Warm air above the shore rises, creating low pressure that pulls cooler ocean air inland—this is your wind.

Thermal sea breeze typically arrives mid-morning, builds steadily through the afternoon, and peaks between 2 and 5 p.m. before dropping off as the sun sets. Wind strength ranges from 8 to 18 knots depending on temperature differential and time of year. Spring and summer produce the strongest thermal sea breezes because land-to-sea temperature contrasts are greatest.

The key to reading a thermal sea breeze is watching the land. On clear, hot days with light synoptic wind, you can almost guarantee an afternoon session. Watch for puffy cumulus clouds forming inland—they signal rising air and stronger thermal activity. The wind direction stays consistent (usually perpendicular to the coastline) because it follows the pressure gradient from sea to land.

Thermal wind is smooth and predictable compared to offshore gusts or storm systems. However, it can be patchy close to shore where land topography and vegetation disrupt flow. Find the sweet spot: far enough from the beach to catch clean wind, but close enough to return easily if conditions fade.

Best Gear for These Conditions

Kite Size: Thermal sea breezes are moderate and steady, making them ideal for mid-range kites. In 10–14 knots, choose a 15–17m kite from Duotone or Cabrinha. If conditions build to 16–18 knots, drop to a 12–15m kite. Avoid oversizing; thermal wind rewards precision, not brute power. A slightly smaller kite gives you better control and lets you read gusts cleanly.

Board: Thermal conditions suit freestyle or all-round directional boards because wind is stable and you'll spend time working tricks or cruising. We recommend a Fanatic or JP Australia freestyle board in the 135–150cm range for lighter riders (under 70kg), or 150–165cm for heavier riders. The flat rocker and responsive feel make these boards perfect for reading subtle wind shifts.

Wetsuit: Thermal wind typically occurs on warm days, but sea temperature still dictates suit choice. In spring (April–May) or autumn (September–October), wear a 3/2mm wetsuit from ION or NeilPryde. Summer sessions may call for just trunks or a 2mm shorty. Never underestimate water temperature—even on hot land days, the sea stays cool.

Technique Tips

Thermal sea breeze rewards smooth, economical movements. The wind is steady but not powerful, so avoid aggressive bar inputs. Instead, make small, purposeful adjustments to your kite height and angle. Keep your kite in the power zone (between 10 and 2 o'clock) and use gentle edge control to generate speed.

Watch for wind lulls close to shore or behind headlands. These are navigation hazards, not tricks opportunities. If you get caught in a lull, ride toward open water where the sea breeze hasn't been disrupted by land features. Conversely, thermal wind often strengthens near rocky outcrops or cliffs where heating is concentrated—scout these zones before committing.

Use the predictability of thermal sea breeze to practise technique. Because the wind is consistent, you can focus on body position, pop timing, and landings without fighting erratic gusts. Build confidence in light conditions; you'll carry that skill into choppier days.

Safety Checklist

  • Verify clear skies: Thermal wind depends on sunshine. Overcast days produce weak or no thermal breeze. Check the forecast for cloud cover and avoid false starts.
  • Watch the sunset window: Thermal wind collapses 30–60 minutes after sunset. Plan your session so you're back on shore by dusk. Being caught offshore in fading wind is dangerous.
  • Scout landing zones: Some thermal breeze spots develop rocky or crowded beaches by afternoon. Know where you'll exit before paddling out.
  • Wear a leash and helmet: Thermal conditions are less intense than storm wind, but falls still happen. Always secure your board and protect your head.
  • Check water temperature: Even in summer, cold water can cause shock and cramp. A wetsuit is non-negotiable—never rely on air temperature alone.
  • Maintain shore contact: Thermal wind can drop without warning. Stay within clear view of shore and within reach of the beach rescue zone.

Our Gear Recommendations at Surf Store

For thermal sea breeze kitesurfing, we stock reliable, proven gear built for consistent conditions. Choose a Duotone or Cabrinha kite in the 12–17m range depending on your weight and local wind strength. These brands excel in light-to-moderate wind and offer intuitive bar feel that suits thermal breezes perfectly.

Pair your kite with a Fanatic, JP Australia, or Nobile freestyle board in the 135–165cm range—the exact size depends on your weight and riding level. All three brands deliver responsive, durable boards that thrive in smooth thermal wind.

Complete your setup with a 3/2mm or 4/3mm wetsuit from ION or NeilPryde. Both brands prioritise warmth without bulk, so you stay comfortable even when water stays cool. Don't forget a helmet and leash—essential safety gear for any session.

Our expert staff have logged hundreds of hours in thermal breeze conditions. Visit surf-store.com to browse our full range, or get in touch with a recommendation tailored to your weight, skill level, and local conditions. We ship across Europe and stand behind every product we sell.

title: Kitesurfing in 20 Knots — Kite Size & Setup Guide
meta_title: Kitesurfing 20 Knots: What Kite Size 2026
meta_description: 20 knots is ideal for progression. Learn kite sizes, techniques & safety for kitesurfing in 20 knots. Find gear at Surf Store.
excerpt: 20 knots offers perfect conditions for learning and progression—here's how to choose your kite size and master the wind.
body_html:
Understanding These Conditions

20 knots is a sweet spot for kitesurfing. It's steady, powerful enough to keep you upwind and progressing tricks, yet not so strong that you're fighting for control. This wind speed sits comfortably between light-wind slogging and demanding high-wind sessions—which is why experienced riders cherish 20-knot days.

At this velocity, the wind is usually consistent and predictable. Gusts may push to 23–25 knots, but the baseline holds firm. These conditions favour intermediate to advanced riders who understand edge control and can adjust their pop and technique on the fly. Beginners on 20 knots often find themselves overpowered, especially on larger kites; if you're new to the sport, you'll want to start in lighter winds and build confidence first.

Water state at 20 knots tends to be choppy but manageable—waves form quickly, chop builds, and the ocean feels alive. This is perfect for freestyle tricks, wave riding practice, and building muscle memory without the fatigue of lighter winds or the danger of stronger gusts.

Best Gear for These Conditions

Kite Size for Kitesurfing in 20 Knots

The best kite size for kitesurfing at 20 knots depends on your weight and riding style:

  • Lighter riders (under 70 kg): 12–14m kites. A 13m from Duotone or Cabrinha gives excellent control and pop for tricks.
  • Medium riders (70–85 kg): 14–15m kites. This range is the goldilocks zone—enough grunt for upwind gains, enough responsiveness for jumps and transitions.
  • Heavier riders (over 85 kg): 15–17m kites. Stick with 15m as your baseline; drop to 14m only if wind climbs to 22+ knots consistently.

Popular brands like Duotone and Cabrinha excel in this range. Their freestyle and wave kites both work well; choose freestyle if you plan tricks, wave if you're chasing swell.

Board Type and Size

At 20 knots, you want a board that floats you easily but still feels responsive underfoot:

  • Freestyle focus: A Nobile or Fanatic freestyle board in the 135–150cm range. These are narrow, designed for tricks, and reward edge pressure.
  • Wave riding: A JP Australia or Tabou wave board, 130–145cm. Slightly more volume forward and a narrower tail for pivoting.
  • All-round progress: A Cabrinha or Point7 hybrid board, 140–155cm. Forgives poor pop, floats in chop, and builds confidence.

Volume and float matter more than length here. If you're progressing jumps or tricks, choose a board that keeps you above the water during slack lines—it saves energy and lets you concentrate on technique.

Wetsuit Thickness

At 20 knots, you'll be in the water for 1–3 hours. Thermal protection is essential:

  • Summer (water 18–22°C): 3/2mm spring suit or 2/2mm summer suit from ION or NeilPryde.
  • Autumn/Spring (water 12–18°C): 4/3mm chest-zip suit. This is your workhorse thickness.
  • Winter (water below 12°C): 5/4mm or hooded 6/5mm. Pair with booties and gloves from ION's winter range.

20-knot sessions demand sustained effort. A ill-fitting or thin suit leads to fatigue, loss of focus, and mistakes—so choose quality over budget.

Technique Tips

At 20 knots, power is available but not excessive. Use it wisely:

  • Work your edge: Lean hard and carve upwind between tricks. The wind supports you; use toe-side edge pressure to build speed.
  • Pop from pressure, not desperation: You don't need to spike your kite or loft aggressively. Instead, build edge pressure, pop smoothly, and let your kite do the work. This builds repeatable technique.
  • Stay centred: In 20 knots, overcommitting to tricks gets punished. Keep your weight neutral, pop from your heels, and use the kite to lift rather than muscle the board.
  • Trim your bar position: Start with your bar at shoulder width. In gusts, let your arms extend slightly; in lulls, bring it closer to maintain tension.
  • Practise smooth transitions: Switch edges fluidly. Jerky weight transfers cause crashes in choppy water—smooth is fast.

Safety Checklist

  • Check the forecast: 20 knots can gust to 25+ without warning. Have an exit plan if wind jumps above 26 knots.
  • Inspect your gear: Look for line fraying, bar cracks, or board delam. 20 knots puts real load on equipment—neglected gear fails here.
  • Know your limits: This wind is not beginner-friendly. If you're learning tricks, start in 12–15 knots first.
  • Ride with a buddy: Chop and stronger wind mean faster tired legs. A friend nearby is a safety net.
  • Wear a helmet and impact vest: Tricks and chop go hand in hand. Head and rib protection are not optional.
  • Stay aware of other water users: Swimmers, paddleboarders, and boats may not expect you in these conditions. Communicate visually or move away.

Our Gear Recommendations at Surf Store

For kitesurfing in 20 knots, we stock everything you need. We recommend:

  • Kites: Duotone or Cabrinha freestyle kites in the 13–15m range, depending on your weight. Both brands offer excellent depower and response at this wind.
  • Boards: Nobile and Fanatic freestyle boards in the 135–150cm range for tricks; JP Australia or Tabou wave boards (130–145cm) if you're chasing swell.
  • Wetsuits: 4/3mm chest-zip from ION or NeilPryde for spring and autumn sessions; 3/2mm spring suit for summer.
  • Hardware: GaastraControl bar systems offer smooth depower. Point7 lines are trusted by our team for durability in choppy water.

Our expert staff test every brand in real conditions. Visit surf-store.com to browse our full range, or contact us for personal advice tailored to your weight, skill level, and local wind patterns.

title: Kitesurfing in 25–30 Knots — Strong Wind Setup Guide
meta_title: Kitesurfing 25–30 Knots: Guide for Strong Wind 2026
meta_description: Master strong-wind kitesurfing in 25–30 knots. Gear, technique, and safety tips from Surf Store experts. Shop now at Surf Store.
excerpt: 25–30 knots is prime strong-wind territory for experienced kiters. Learn the right gear, technique, and safety measures to ride these conditions confidently.
body_html:
Understanding These Conditions

Kitesurfing in 25–30 knots sits firmly in the strong wind category. This is where things get serious: the water becomes choppy, gusts become pronounced, and your kite generates genuine power. For most intermediate and advanced riders, these conditions are where you really start to feel the energy. Wind in this range is common across Europe during autumn and winter, particularly on exposed beaches and coastal bays.

At 25–30 knots, you're dealing with consistent pull from your kite, which means faster speeds, bigger jumps, and—if you're not careful—sudden direction changes when gusts hit. The water state ranges from choppy to rough depending on fetch and local geography. This is not beginner territory, but it's the sweet spot for riders who want to progress tricks, perfect their edge control, and build confidence in challenging conditions.

Best Gear for These Conditions

In 25–30 knots, your kite size is critical. Most riders will be comfortable on a 12–14m kite from brands like Duotone, Cabrinha, or NeilPryde. If you're on the heavier side or prefer a more relaxed ride, a 15m works too—but don't go larger unless you have genuine experience in strong wind. Smaller kites (9–11m) are for the upper end of the range or expert riders hunting maximum speed.

Board choice matters enormously. A freestyle or all-round board in the 135–150cm range gives you control and response without being unwieldy. Brands like Nobile, JP Australia, and Fanatic make boards that excel in choppy water. If you're riding waves, a directional board in the 130–145cm range from Point7 or JP Australia will feel more natural.

For wetsuits, go thicker. A 4/3mm or 5/3mm suit from ION or NeilPryde will keep you warm during extended sessions and provide better support when you're being tossed around in chop. In summer, a 3/2mm will suffice, but be prepared to cut sessions short if the wind stays up and fatigue sets in.

Technique Tips

In kitesurfing in 25–30 knots, your stance and weight distribution change. Keep your weight back and down—let your legs absorb the chop rather than fighting it. Your kite control must be sharper: smaller stick inputs, quicker responses to gusts. Don't overcommit to tricks until you've felt the conditions for 15–20 minutes.

Edging is your friend. Build your speed slowly and lean hard on your toe or heel edge to feel the water. When you're ready to jump, anticipate gusts and use them; don't fight them. Keep your bar closer to your body to maintain tension and prevent slack lines—slack lines in strong wind are dangerous and hard to recover from.

Throttle your power. You don't need to max out your kite's angle; stay slightly back from zenith (the highest point) to maintain control. When riding upwind, angle your board more and drive harder with your legs; the wind is doing the work, so don't tire yourself fighting it.

Safety Checklist

  • Always use a safety release. A leash or quick-release system is non-negotiable in 25–30 knots. If you lose control, you need to be able to dump your kite immediately.
  • Check your bar and lines. Look for fraying, weak spots, or damaged stitching before you launch. Strong wind magnifies equipment failure.
  • Launch and land with a helper. Never launch solo in these conditions. A buddy keeps your kite stable during setup and collapse.
  • Stay away from crowded zones. In strong wind, your control radius expands; choose a quiet beach or sideshore spot where you won't risk other riders.
  • Know your limits. If you're exhausted, get off the water. Fatigue kills technique and decision-making in strong wind.
  • Wear a helmet and impact vest. Falls from jumps in choppy water can be violent. Protect your head and ribs.
  • Watch the forecast closely. Strong wind often brings gusts 5–10 knots above the average. If the forecast is 30 knots with gusts to 40, consider sitting it out.

Our Gear Recommendations at Surf Store

For kitesurfing in 25–30 knots, we stock everything you need. Choose a 12–15m kite from Duotone, Cabrinha, or NeilPryde—these brands are trusted by our team for reliability and power delivery in strong wind. Pair it with a freestyle or all-round board from Nobile, JP Australia, or Fanatic in the 135–150cm range; these excel in choppy water and give you the control you need when the wind gets rowdy.

For wetsuits, we recommend ION or NeilPryde 4/3mm or 5/3mm suits depending on the season and water temperature where you ride. A thicker suit keeps you warm, reduces fatigue, and protects you when you take hard falls in chop. Don't skimp on this—a good wetsuit is as important as your kite in strong conditions.

Visit Surf Store to browse our full range of Duotone, Cabrinha, Nobile, JP Australia, Fanatic, ION, and NeilPryde kites, boards, and wetsuits. Our team rides in these conditions regularly and can help you pick the exact combination for your size, skill level, and local wind patterns. We ship across Europe and are here to make sure you're set up for success.

title: Kitesurfing in 12–15 Knots — Light Wind Tips & Gear
meta_title: Kitesurfing Light Wind 12–15 Knots: Tips & Gear 2026
meta_description: Master kitesurfing light wind 12–15 knots with our expert guide on kite sizes, technique, and safety. Find gear at Surf Store.
excerpt: Learn how to kitesurfing light wind 12–15 knots conditions with the right kite size, technique adjustments, and safety practices.
body_html:
Understanding These Conditions

Kitesurfing light wind 12–15 knots sits in the tricky zone between ideal and underpowered. At the lower end of this range, you'll feel the kite working hard to generate power, and your board will demand constant pressure to maintain momentum. By 15 knots, you'll have more glide and control, but still need a bigger kite and a more forgiving board than you would in medium to strong winds.

These marginal conditions are common on European lakes, sheltered coastal spots, and during shoulder seasons. Light wind doesn't mean bad conditions—it means different technique and gear choices. Many riders actually prefer this range because it forces better board control and kite handling skills.

Best Gear for These Conditions

Kite size is your biggest priority in kitesurfing light wind 12–15 knots. You'll want to go larger than usual: a 14–17m kite from Duotone or Cabrinha will give you the projected area needed to generate consistent lift. If you're heavier or prefer freestyle tricks, consider the top end of that range; lighter riders can lean toward 14–15m.

Your board should be larger and more volume-forward than standard. Look for a freestyle or freeride board in the 150–165cm range—brands like Nobile, Fanatic, and JP Australia all make boards designed for light wind that won't sink or drag. The extra volume helps you stay afloat and moving without needing aggressive kite pressure.

Wetsuits in this season-dependent wind are typically 3/2mm or 4/3mm depending on your location and water temperature. Spring and autumn sessions may lean toward 4/3mm (ION or NeilPryde); summer sessions in 12–15 knots might use a 3/2mm. Don't skimp here—staying warm means better focus and longer sessions.

Technique Tips

Light wind demands active kite work. Keep your kite constantly moving through the power zone—between 12 and 2 o'clock—rather than letting it drift. Small figure-eight motions and smooth redirects generate apparent wind and keep you powered.

Use heel-edge pressure more than toe edge. Lean back slightly more than in stronger wind to keep your board moving, and avoid over-sheeting your bar. Let the kite do the work; jerky, aggressive movements waste energy in light conditions.

Commit to your tacks and jibes completely. Slow, indecisive turns will kill momentum in kitesurfing light wind 12–15 knots. When you initiate a turn, move through it with purpose and keep your body weight forward to maintain board speed.

Practice riding with minimal bar pressure. This teaches kite control and makes the transition to stronger winds feel natural once they arrive.

Safety Checklist

  • Check your harness and lines regularly. Light-wind sessions can be long, and fatigue increases risk. Worn lines are harder to manage when you need precise kite control.
  • Know your exit route. Light wind can trap you—if conditions drop further, drift can become an issue. Always have a clear path back to shore or an easy water-exit plan.
  • Avoid over-hooking in your harness. In light wind, keep your movements free and unhooked more often. This prevents surprise crashes if the kite suddenly catches air.
  • Use a rescue rig or have a buddy nearby. Calm water and light wind can feel safe, but if you lose control or equipment fails, rescue is essential. Never kite alone in marginal conditions.
  • Monitor wind direction shifts. Light wind can be unstable. A sudden gust or lull might catch you unaware; stay alert and ready to ditch.

Our Gear Recommendations at Surf Store

At Surf Store, we specialise in kitesurfing light wind 12–15 knots setups. We stock large kites from Duotone and Cabrinha in the 14–17m range, which are our go-to brands for reliable power generation in marginal conditions. Both brands offer excellent bar feel and relaunch characteristics when you need to recover from a dunking.

For boards, we recommend Fanatic, Nobile, and JP Australia freestyle and freeride models in the 150–165cm range. These boards are designed to hold shape and float well in light wind without feeling sluggish in stronger gusts.

Wetsuits from ION and NeilPryde in 3/2mm or 4/3mm will keep you comfortable during long light-wind sessions. Both brands offer excellent mobility and durability.

Visit surf-store.com to browse our complete range, check sizing guides, and chat with our team. We have real on-water experience and can help you match the right setup to your weight, skill level, and local conditions.

title: Windsurfing in 20 Knots — Sail Size & Setup Guide
meta_title: Windsurfing 20 Knots Sail Size Setup 2026 | Surf Store
meta_description: Expert guide to windsurfing in 20 knots: ideal sail sizes, boards, and technique. Find your perfect setup at Surf Store.
excerpt: 20 knots is ideal windsurfing conditions. Learn the right sail size, board choice, and technique to make the most of moderate wind.
body_html:
Understanding These Conditions

20 knots sits in the sweet spot for most windsurfers—consistent enough to generate real power, yet manageable for intermediate and advanced riders. This is the wind speed where windsurfing truly comes alive: you'll have enough leverage for tricks, jumps, and wave riding without being overpowered or struggling to stay upright.

At 20 knots, you're looking at wind that's steady and predictable, often with some chop on the water but not dangerous conditions. Gusts might spike to 25 knots, while lulls may drop to 15, so understanding how to adjust your stance and sail trim becomes crucial. This is also the wind range where most Europeans find their local spots most enjoyable—enough energy to progress, but not so extreme that only experts feel comfortable.

Best Gear for Windsurfing 20 Knots Sail Size Setup

When sailing in 20 knots, sail size is your primary control lever. Most windsurfers in these conditions use sails in the 4.5–5.8m² range, depending on rider weight and board type:

  • Lighter riders (under 70kg): A 5.0–5.5m² wave or freeride sail gives you comfort and control without tiring your arms.
  • Heavier riders (70–90kg): A 5.5–6.5m² all-round or freeride sail balances power and manoeuvrability.
  • Very heavy riders (over 90kg): A 6.5–7.5m² sail may be needed, though you'll want good footwork to keep things smooth.

We recommend Duotone, NeilPryde, and Gaastra wave or freeride sails in these sizes—they're designed for the power delivery and durability you need at 20 knots.

For boards, choose a wave board or freeride board in the 70–85 litre range (roughly 240–265cm length). Brands like JP Australia, Fanatic, and Tabou excel here. Smaller boards offer quicker response and easier tricks; larger boards provide stability and help you maintain speed in lulls.

Wetsuit thickness depends on your location and season. In warmer months, a 3/2mm suit from ION or NeilPryde keeps you protected without overheating. As water temperature drops (autumn/winter in Northern Europe), upgrade to 4/3mm or 5/4mm to stay warm during longer sessions.

Technique Tips

At 20 knots, your sail trim matters more than raw power. Keep your boom height moderate—not too high, as this closes off your sail and creates luffing. Maintain a slight bend in your knees and keep your weight centred over the board's sweet spot.

Use sheet-in and sheet-out movements to control power rather than leaning back. Small adjustments to sail angle often prevent crashes better than brute strength. When windsurfing in these conditions, focus on smooth transitions: rolling your hips, keeping your front arm slightly bent, and letting the sail do the work.

For wave riding, use the natural power from 20 knots to generate speed, then feather the sail upwind to maintain control. For freestyle or light jumping, the consistent breeze lets you commit fully to moves without worrying about wind dropout.

Safety Checklist

  • Check your equipment: Inspect mast, boom, and battens for damage before launch. A loose batten at 20 knots can escalate problems quickly.
  • Use a leash: Always attach your board to your harness with a wrist or waist leash, especially if you're practising tricks.
  • Know your limits: 20 knots is forgiving, but don't attempt moves beyond your skill level. Build progressively.
  • Watch weather changes: Gusts can surprise you. Keep an eye on the sky and be ready to dump power by easing your sheet hand.
  • Dress for immersion: Even with a wetsuit, unexpected duckdives or falls happen. Ensure your suit provides adequate protection for your local water temperature.
  • Buddy system: Never windsurf alone, especially if practising new techniques.

Our Gear Recommendations at Surf Store

We stock everything you need for windsurfing in 20 knots. For sails, we carry Duotone, NeilPryde, and Gaastra wave and freeride sails in the 4.5–7.5m² range—choose based on your weight and the tricks you want to progress.

Our board selection includes JP Australia, Fanatic, and Tabou wave and freeride boards in the 70–85 litre range, suited for quick turning and stability in steady wind. Pair your sail and board with a 3/2mm or 4/3mm wetsuit from ION or NeilPryde depending on your local water temperature and season.

All our gear is backed by real on-water experience—our team uses these exact sails and boards in European conditions year-round. Visit surf-store.com or contact us directly for personalised recommendations based on your weight, skill level, and local spot.

title: Windsurfing in 30 Knots — Strong Wind Tips & Gear
meta_title: Windsurfing Strong Wind 30 Knots Tips 2026
meta_description: Master windsurfing in strong wind with expert 30-knot guides, gear picks, and technique tips. Shop quality sails and boards at Surf Store.
excerpt: 30-knot conditions demand the right sail size, board control, and safety awareness—here's how to read the conditions and thrive.
body_html:
Understanding These Conditions

30 knots is strong, choppy wind — well beyond intermediate territory. At this speed, your windsurfing strong wind 30 knots experience becomes a test of board control, core strength, and quick decision-making. The water surface is rough with frequent whitecaps, the sail load is intense, and mistakes happen faster. Most riders stepping into 30-knot conditions for the first time feel immediately how much heavier everything becomes. This is where technique separates confident sailors from those who tire quickly.

These conditions favour lighter, athletic riders with solid wave or freestyle experience. Heavier sailors often need to upsize their boards and downsize their sails further. Wind is rarely stable at 30 knots — you'll experience gusts pushing 35+ knots followed by lulls, so your rig choice must handle that range without forcing a sail change mid-session.

Best Gear for These Conditions

Sails: Move down to 13–15m sails. Most 30-knot windsurfing demands a 14m or 15m, depending on your weight and strength. Lighter riders (under 70kg) may handle a 12–13m if they're comfortable; heavier riders (80kg+) typically stick with 14–15m. Choose a freewave or wave sail rather than pure freestyle — these sails have a flatter profile, easier handling, and better control in choppy water. Duotone and Cabrinha both make excellent freewave and wave sails suited to this wind strength.

Boards: Select a freewave or wave board in the 80–100cm range, or a high-wind freestyle/wave hybrid in the 100–120cm range if you prefer more volume and pop. These boards are narrower, have sharper rails, and respond instantly to pressure — exactly what you need in strong, gusty conditions. Nobile, JP Australia, and Fanatic specialise in boards built for this intensity.

Booms: Use a boom 160–200cm depending on sail size and your reach. A shorter boom increases responsiveness; a longer boom offers more leverage on your feet. At 30 knots, precision matters more than extra length.

Wetsuits: Water temperature and air conditions vary across Europe, but generally choose a 3/2mm or 4/3mm winter suit. If you're in the Atlantic or North Sea in autumn/winter, a 5/4mm or even a 6/5mm may be necessary. ION and NeilPryde make robust wetsuits with reinforced panels designed to handle the punishment of high-wind sessions. Thicker gloves and booties reduce hand/foot fatigue.

Technique Tips

Foot positioning: Keep your feet wider apart and further back on the board than you would in lighter wind. This lowers your centre of gravity and gives you more leverage over the tail during manoeuvres and when riding through gusts.
