title: Wetsuit Thickness by Water Temperature — Full Guide 2026
meta_title: Wetsuit Thickness Water Temperature Guide 2026
meta_description: Match wetsuit thickness to water temperature with our practical guide chart. Learn when to wear 3/2mm, 4/3mm, 5/4mm suits. Shop now at Surf Store.
excerpt: Choose wetsuit thickness by matching millimetre rating to your water temperature: 3/2mm for 16–18°C, 4/3mm for 12–16°C, 5/4mm for 8–12°C, 6/5mm for below 8°C.
body_html:
Choosing the right wetsuit thickness by water temperature is straightforward once you know the basic rule: thicker neoprene insulates better in colder water, thinner suits work in warmer conditions. The numbers you see (like 3/2mm or 4/3mm) represent chest/limb thickness, and matching them to your local water temperature is the fastest way to stay comfortable, mobile, and safe on the water all year.

The Full Answer

Water temperature, not air temperature, drives your wetsuit thickness choice. A 15°C sea in winter feels very different from a 15°C lake in spring, and your body loses heat rapidly in cold water—much faster than in air. The wetsuit thickness water temperature guide chart is the industry standard tool, and it's worth learning once because you'll use it for kitesurfing, windsurfing, wing foiling, and SUP.

The thickness notation works like this: a 3/2mm suit has 3mm neoprene on the torso (where your core loses most heat) and 2mm on the arms and legs (where you need more mobility). Thicker suits trap more warm water against your skin, but they also reduce flexibility and buoyancy—important if you're learning freestyle tricks or need quick paddling.

Most water sports athletes change suits seasonally. Rather than buy one mega-thick winter suit, many pros and serious enthusiasts own 2–3 suits: a thin spring/autumn suit, a mid-weight winter suit, and sometimes a shorty or spring suit for summer. This approach gives you comfort without sacrificing performance.

Practical Guide

Water Temperature to Wetsuit Thickness Chart:

Water Temperature Recommended Thickness Suit Type Best Season
18–24°C 1.5–2mm or none Shorty or rash guard Summer
14–18°C 3/2mm Spring/autumn full suit Spring & autumn
10–14°C 4/3mm or 5/3mm Winter full suit Late autumn & early spring
6–10°C 5/4mm or 6/5mm Thick winter full suit Winter
Below 6°C 6/5mm + boots, gloves, hood Maximum insulation suit Deep winter

How to measure your water temperature: Use a floating thermometer or check local surf/wind forecasts—most include sea temperature data. Don't guess based on air temperature alone; a sunny winter day can have ice-cold water.

Step-by-step decision:

  • Check your local water temperature forecast
  • Find the matching range in the chart above
  • If you're on the boundary (e.g. 14°C), choose the thicker option if you're sensitive to cold or plan long sessions
  • Test fit the suit—you should have a finger's width of space inside the neck and shoulders, not baggy or painfully tight
  • Consider your personal cold tolerance: women and lighter athletes often go one thickness up

Common Mistakes

1. Choosing thickness based on air temperature only. You can be in bright sunshine with 15°C water and freeze in a shorty. Always check actual water temperature.

2. Buying one mega-thick suit for all seasons. A 6/5mm suit in summer is miserable—restrictive, overheats you before you hit the water, and you'll abandon it. Two suits (3/2mm and 5/4mm) give you year-round comfort.

3. Ignoring accessories in very cold water. Even a thick full suit loses heat at the extremities. Below 6°C, add neoprene boots, gloves, and a hood; they're essential, not optional.

4. Assuming all brands fit the same. Chest width, shoulder mobility, and ankle/wrist openings vary. Try before you buy—or know your usual brand's fit pattern.

Surf Store Recommendation

We stock winter and season-round wetsuits from ION, Gaastra, and other trusted brands. title: Kite Size by Weight & Wind Speed — Full Calculator Guide
meta_title: Kite Size Calculator by Weight & Wind Speed 2026
meta_description: Learn to choose the right kite size using our weight and wind speed chart. Expert guidance for kitesurfers. Shop now at Surf Store.
excerpt: Match your kite size to your body weight and local wind conditions using a simple chart and three-step formula for safe, responsive riding.
body_html:
Kite size depends on your body weight and the wind speed at your location. As a general rule, lighter riders need smaller kites in strong wind, while heavier riders need larger kites in light wind. Use a kite size calculator chart that cross-references your weight (in kilograms) with wind speed (in knots) to find your ideal kite size range, typically between 7m² and 19m².

The Full Answer

Choosing the right kite size by weight and wind speed is the foundation of safe and enjoyable kitesurfing. Every rider has a "sweet spot" – a combination of kite size and wind conditions where they feel in control, generate enough power to ride, and can safely manage the board and bar.

A kite size calculator works by matching two variables: your body weight and the apparent wind speed you're experiencing on the water. Heavier riders displace more energy from the wind, so they need larger kites to generate enough lift and power. Lighter riders feel more wind pressure, so smaller kites prevent them being overpowered.

Wind speed affects kite performance exponentially. A 2-knot increase in wind speed roughly doubles the apparent power you feel on the bar. This means your ideal kite size shrinks as conditions strengthen – and grows as wind fades.

Practical Guide

Step 1: Know Your Weight
Write down your body weight in kilograms. This is your starting point.

Step 2: Check Wind Speed
Learn to read wind forecasts in knots (most water sports forecasts use knots, not km/h). Apps like Windy or your local spot's forecast will give you true wind speed. Remember that wind on the water is often 1–3 knots stronger than forecast.

Step 3: Use a Kite Size Calculator Chart
Match your weight (vertical axis) with wind speed (horizontal axis). Here's a typical framework for adult riders:

Body Weight (kg) 10–12 knots 13–16 knots 17–20 knots 21+ knots
55–70 kg 14–17m² 12–14m² 10–12m² 7–10m²
70–85 kg 17–19m² 14–17m² 12–14m² 10–12m²
85–100 kg 19m²+ 17–19m² 14–17m² 12–14m²

Step 4: Consider Your Skill Level
Beginners should aim for the upper end of their size range to make launching, riding, and landing easier. Intermediates and advanced riders can use smaller kites in stronger wind for more control and trick potential.

Step 5: Own Multiple Kites
Most serious kitesurfers own 2–3 kites to cover a wider range of conditions. A 12m² and 17m² kite combination works well for many European spots where wind varies seasonally.

Common Mistakes

  • Ignoring apparent wind. Wind speed on the water is stronger when you're moving. Beginners often choose kites too large, thinking they need maximum power in light wind.
  • Confusing kite size with board size. Kite size and board size are independent choices. A 14m² kite pairs with different board sizes depending on your weight and the tricks you want to land.
  • Not accounting for local wind patterns. A kite size calculator gives general guidance, but your local spot's wind strength and consistency matter. Visit the spot on forecast days to learn what works.
  • Sticking with one kite. A single kite size rarely covers the full year in Europe. Plan to invest in two kites for consistent riding.
  • Underestimating gust and lull. Wind is never constant. Always choose a kite size that handles 2–3 knots of variation safely.

Surf Store Recommendation

At Surf Store, we stock complete kite quivers from top brands including Duotone, Cabrinha, NeilPryde, and Gaastra. We recommend:

  • For beginners: A 17m² kite from Duotone or Cabrinha. These sizes are forgiving, launch easily, and work across a range of wind speeds.
  • For intermediate to advanced riders: A 14m² and 10m² combination from Duotone, Cabrinha, or NeilPryde. This pairing covers light winds through strong conditions and allows you to progress trick skills.
  • Heavier riders (90+ kg): Consider a 19m² as your light-wind kite paired with a 12–14m² for moderate conditions.

Our team has real on-water experience and can help you match a kite to your weight, local wind patterns, and goals. Visit us or contact our experts to discuss your ideal kite size calculator profile. Shop our complete kite range at Surf Store – we ship across Europe.

title: Kite Foiling in Light Wind — Minimum Wind & Technique Guide
meta_title: Kite Foiling Light Wind Guide 2026 | Surf Store
meta_description: Master kite foiling light wind techniques with the right gear. Learn minimum speed, board selection & safety tips. Shop now at Surf Store.
excerpt: Kite foiling light wind sessions demand the right combination of large kites, lightweight foil boards, and precise body positioning to generate lift and main...
body_html:
Understanding These Conditions

Light wind kite foiling typically occurs between 8–14 knots, where traditional kiteboarding struggles but foiling shines. This is the sweet spot where a hydrofoil's lift generation allows you to stay upwind and mobile on far less pressure than a conventional board requires. Light wind is common in early morning sessions, sheltered bays, and transition periods between strong wind days—making it a skill worth mastering if you want to maximise your water time.

The challenge lies in understanding that kite foiling light wind performance depends heavily on three factors: board volume and foil design, kite size and responsiveness, and your own body weight and technique. Unlike powered-up conditions where brute force works, light wind kite foiling demands finesse, weight distribution, and reading the smallest pressure changes in the kite. Wind tends to be lighter and more turbulent near shore, so paddling out to cleaner air often makes the difference between a frustrating session and a flowing one.

Best Gear for These Conditions

For kite foiling light wind, board choice is critical. You'll want a kite foil board in the 160–180cm range with high volume to provide flotation and early lift. Boards like those from Fanatic, JP Australia, or Nobile in this size range are designed specifically to make light-wind foiling accessible. The larger volume keeps you on the foil longer during lulls and helps you generate enough speed to maintain flight.

Your foil setup matters equally. Foils with larger front wing surface areas and longer mast lengths are your friend in light wind kite foiling light wind sessions. A 75cm mast paired with a large front wing (typically 1000–1200cm²) will lift earlier and hold you higher than aggressive race foils. Many brands now offer dedicated light-wind foil packages that balance responsiveness with forgiveness.

Kite size is where most riders go wrong in light wind. A 17m or 19m kite from Duotone, Cabrinha, or NeilPryde is standard for these conditions. At 10–12 knots, a 19m will give you the depower range and power delivery you need without over-rotating. Modern freestyle and wave kites in these sizes are more manoeuvrable than older designs, so you're not sacrificed control for size. Avoid undersizing; a 14m or 15m might feel sharper, but you'll spend half your session trying to generate pressure.

Wetsuits should be 3/2mm or 4/3mm depending on season and water temperature. ION and NeilPryde both make excellent thermal protection that won't restrict your kite and board control. Light-wind sessions often mean longer water time, so comfort is worth the investment.

Technique Tips

The foundation of kite foiling light wind success is aggressive kite steering without overstressing the bar. In light wind, every repositioning of the kite is a power-building opportunity. Make wide, controlled loops through your power zone (roughly 11–1 o'clock) rather than sharp cuts. This keeps tension in the line and builds wave energy into your kite, translating to sustained lift on the foil board.

Body position is everything. Sit deeper on the board than you would in powered conditions—your weight further back helps the foil maintain angle of attack as wind pressure drops. Keep your knees bent and your hips mobile; you'll need to weight and unweight the board constantly to maintain foil flight through micro-gusts and lulls. Think of light-wind foiling as a continuous conversation between your body and the foil, not a locked-in stance.

When you feel the kite lose pressure, immediately generate board speed by carving downwind slightly, then redirect back on your heading once you've regained momentum. Never just sit and hope; constant input is what keeps you flying in marginal wind. Pump the board gently between gusts—this isn't aggressive jump-pumping, but rather rhythm and timing with the available pressure.

Finally, position yourself in the cleanest wind available. Move away from obstacles, land masses, and chop that interrupt wind flow. Even 50 metres offshore can mean noticeably steadier gusts, making the difference between frustration and progression.

Safety Checklist

  • Launch and land carefully: Light wind means reduced bar tension, making the kite less predictable during inflation. Always use a proper launch technique and have a buddy or use an inflatable launch pad.
  • Watch for sudden lulls: You may foil beautifully, then lose all pressure in seconds. Stay alert and have a bailout plan—know how to step off the board cleanly and swim back to shore or your starting point.
  • Check your leash: A solid leash connection is essential so you don't lose your board if you fall off the foil unexpectedly.
  • Helmet and impact vest are non-negotiable: Foil crashes can happen at low speeds, and water can come up fast when you're learning light-wind technique.
  • Stay within sight of shore: Light wind sessions often take longer, and conditions can deteriorate. Never venture too far from safety or assistance.
  • Check your kite and bar before launch: Inspect all bridle lines, the leading edge, and bar connections. Worn gear fails faster in the sustained tension of light-wind sessions.

Our Gear Recommendations at Surf Store

We stock everything you need to master kite foiling light wind at Surf Store. For boards, we recommend Fanatic, JP Australia, or Nobile foil boards in the 160–180cm range, all proven performers in marginal wind and proven by our own staff on European waters.

Pair your board with a 17m or 19m kite from Duotone, Cabrinha, or NeilPryde—all brands we trust for reliability and progressive design. These kites deliver the low-end grunt and depower range that makes light-wind foiling feel connected, not chaotic.

For wetsuits, ION and NeilPryde 3/2mm or 4/3mm suits will keep you warm and mobile through longer sessions. And don't overlook a quality impact vest; safety is part of the fun, not a compromise.

Our team at Surf Store has real experience on foils in all European wind ranges. Visit us online or get in touch—we're happy to discuss board volumes, foil setups, and kite sizes matched to your weight and local conditions. Let's get you foiling in light wind this season.

title: Foil vs Regular Kiteboard in Light Wind — Which Is Better?
meta_title: Foil vs Regular Kiteboard Light Wind 2026
meta_description: Discover whether a foil or regular kiteboard performs better in light wind. Expert guidance for kitesurfers. Shop at Surf Store.
excerpt: Foil kiteboards are superior in light wind because they generate lift earlier and require less power, making them ideal when conditions are marginal.
body_html:
In light wind, a foil kiteboard is almost always the better choice. Foils generate hydrodynamic lift at lower speeds, letting you stay airborne and upwind in conditions where regular boards would sink. If you're riding in winds below 12 knots or in gusty, patchy conditions, a foil will unlock riding sessions that would otherwise be impossible on a standard twin-tip or directional board.

The Full Answer

The difference between foil and regular kiteboards in light wind comes down to physics. A foil (a hydrofoil wing submerged beneath the board) creates lift as water passes over it, similar to how an aircraft wing works. This means you can generate forward momentum and stay flying at remarkably low speeds—sometimes as little as 8–10 knots of true wind, depending on your weight and kite size.

A regular kiteboard—whether a freestyle twin-tip or directional wave board—relies entirely on flotation and drag. Once you slow down below the board's natural water speed, you sink and lose power. In light, inconsistent wind, this happens constantly, turning a session into a series of stops and starts rather than flowing, uninterrupted riding.

For light-wind foil versus regular kiteboard comparisons, the foil wins on one critical metric: minimum wind speed. Regulars need steady 12+ knots; foils thrive at 8–12 knots and can work even lighter in strong gusts. That's the reason foil riders are out in conditions where regular riders are watching from the beach.

Practical Guide

Choose a foil if:

  • Your local spot sees frequent winds under 12 knots
  • You want to maximise session frequency in variable conditions
  • You're willing to invest in learning a new technique (foiling has a learning curve)
  • You already have experience on a regular kiteboard

Stick with a regular kiteboard if:

  • Your area consistently has 13+ knots or stronger
  • You're a beginner—learn fundamentals on a stable twin-tip first
  • You prefer the confidence and durability of a traditional board
  • You want to avoid the cost of a full foil system (board + mast + fuselage)

Light-wind session strategy: If you're already a solid kite pilot on a regular board and you're tired of missing light-wind days, invest in a foil setup. Start with a mid-range foil in the 1200–1400 cm² wing area, which offers forgiving handling and good glide. Spend 5–10 sessions getting comfortable before comparing it directly to your old board.

Common Mistakes

New foilers often overestimate their light-wind advantage by choosing a wing that's too small. A 900 cm² foil in 10 knots is harder to ride than a 1200 cm² foil in the same wind—don't let brand marketing convince you otherwise. Foils still need *some* wind to work; they're not magic, just more efficient.

Another mistake is comparing foil performance to regular kiteboards in moderate wind (14+ knots). In that range, both work fine, and the foil's advantage narrows. The real win for foils is *below* 12 knots, where regular boards stop performing altogether.

Beginners also underestimate the handling difference. Foils require more precise kite control and board awareness than a forgiving twin-tip. If you're new to kitesurfing, master a regular board first, then graduate to foil after 30+ hours on the water.

Surf Store Recommendation

For light-wind riding across Europe, we stock foil systems and boards from market leaders Duotone, Cabrinha, NeilPryde, and JP Australia. If you're deciding between foil and regular boards, our experienced team can advise you based on your local wind patterns and skill level. We carry freestyle and all-round twin-tips from Duotone, Cabrinha, and Nobile in a range of sizes (130–150 cm), plus foil setups in various wing sizes to suit light-wind and cross-over riding. Visit surf-store.com to compare options or reach out with your location and average wind speeds—we'll recommend what makes sense for your conditions.

title: Directional vs Twin-Tip in Waves — Which Kiteboard to Choose?
meta_title: Directional vs Twin-Tip Kiteboard in Waves 2026
meta_description: Choose between directional and twin-tip kiteboards for wave riding. Expert guide on which works best for your skill level. Find it at Surf Store.
excerpt: For serious wave riding, a directional kiteboard gives better control and speed, whilst twin-tips suit freestyle and variable wind conditions.
body_html:
In waves, a directional kiteboard will give you better turning control, more hold on the wave face, and genuine surf feel—making it the first choice for serious wave riding. A twin-tip works in choppy inland water and windy beach breaks, but lacks the drive and rail hold of a directional board when you're actually riding proper swell.

The Full Answer

The choice between a directional kiteboard and a twin-tip comes down to where and how you ride. A directional kiteboard is shaped with a defined nose and tail, deeper rails, and more volume forward. This design lets you carve across the wave face, hold an edge in the pocket, and generate real speed off the bottom turn. You'll feel like you're surfing, because you are.

A twin-tip is symmetrical end-to-end, with softer edges and a park-style flex. It's forgiving, versatile across wind speeds, and brilliant for freestyle tricks and flat-water cruising. But in waves, that soft edge and central volume distribution work against you. You'll struggle to hold a line on the face, lose your edge in turns, and feel like you're skidding rather than carving.

If you're in a genuine wave zone—exposed coasts, reef breaks, or rolling beach swell—invest in a directional kiteboard. If you're learning in choppy bay water or shallow lagoons with inconsistent conditions, a twin-tip buys you forgiveness and options.

Practical Guide

Choose directional if:

  • You have regular access to real wave breaks (Atlantic coasts, Mediterranean exposed beaches, strong swell periods).
  • You want to land proper turns and follow the wave's energy down the line.
  • You're willing to spend more and maintain a wave-specific setup.
  • You have enough wind to stay upwind (directional boards work in 12+ knots with skill).

Choose twin-tip if:

  • You're learning in choppy, enclosed water or flat lagoons.
  • Wind is often light or gusty, and you want a board that forgives slack lines.
  • You want one board that handles freestyle, cruising, and small waves equally.
  • You're not yet committed to wave riding as your primary discipline.

Size matters too. A directional wave board in the 135–155cm range gives you float and manoeuvrability. Heavier riders (80+ kg) should look toward the upper end; lighter riders (under 65 kg) can go smaller. Twin-tips typically run 136–145cm for most adults.

Common Mistakes

Mistake 1: Buying a directional board too small. Wave boards need enough volume to support you in light wind and hold you on the face. A 135cm directional board won't work for a 90kg rider in 12-knot winds. Oversize slightly—you'll actually have more control.

Mistake 2: Expecting a twin-tip to carve like a directional. It won't. Twin-tips are fast and fun, but they pivot on their centre, not their rails. Stop expecting wave performance from a park board.

Mistake 3: Not testing before you buy. Your weight, wind reliability, wave quality, and personal style all matter. A board that works for your mate might be wrong for you. Chat with an instructor before spending €500+.

Mistake 4: Mixing disciplines without clarity. If you try to use one board for waves, freestyle, and flat-water cruising, you'll compromise everywhere. Pick your priority and choose accordingly.

Surf Store Recommendation

For genuine wave riding, we stock directional wave boards from Duotone, Cabrinha, and NeilPryde in the 135–160cm range, depending on your weight and local conditions. Each brand brings its own character—Cabrinha excels in powerful reef and beach breaks, while Duotone offers playful, accessible designs for all levels.

If you're unsure whether to go directional or want a do-everything board, our twin-tip selection from Cabrinha and Nobile delivers solid performance in choppy water and light wind.

We recommend visiting us or contacting our team with details of your location, weight, local wind and wave patterns, and riding goals. We'll steer you toward the right directional kiteboard or twin-tip for your setup. Head to surf-store.com to browse our range or book a consultation with our instructors.

title: Kite Harness Setup for Big Air & Jumping — Settings Guide
meta_title: Kite Harness for Jumping Big Air 2026 | Surf Store
meta_description: Master big air jumping with proper kite harness settings. Learn technique, gear, and safety for massive airs. Find it at Surf Store.
excerpt: Discover the essential kite harness adjustments, technique, and gear needed to land massive big air jumps safely and consistently.
body_html:
Understanding These Conditions

Big air kitesurfing demands specific environmental and technical conditions to execute safely and consistently. You're looking for steady wind in the 14–20+ knot range, ideally from a direction that keeps you clear of obstacles and allows long, uninterrupted runs downwind. The water state matters too: smaller chop is actually preferable to large, irregular waves when you're focusing on height rather than wave riding. Your kite harness settings become critical because they determine how quickly you can build edge pressure, load the kite, and generate the explosive pop needed for airtime.

The best sessions occur when wind is relatively consistent without massive gusts or lulls. Gusty conditions can throw off your timing mid-jump, while light, patchy wind makes it hard to generate enough lift. A solid 16–18 knot window with light chop and good visibility is the sweet spot for dialling in your technique and pushing vertical limits.

Best Gear for These Conditions

Your kite size is the foundation of big air success. For consistent jumping in 14–18 knots, you'll want a 13–15m kite from Duotone or Cabrinha—these brands excel at delivering the responsive, powerful turn you need to load and pop. In stronger winds (18–20+ knots), drop to a 11–13m kite to maintain control during the air phase. Avoid oversizing; an oversized kite makes it harder to manage bar pressure and increases the risk of blown-out landings.

For your board, a freestyle or twin-tip board in the 135–150cm range from Nobile, Fanatic, or Cabrinha provides the pop and edge hold essential for jumping. These sizes strike the right balance between flotation and manoeuvrability. Lighter riders (under 75kg) might favour the smaller end; heavier riders should look towards 145–150cm. The board's edge and rail design are more important than absolute size when you're focused on vertical rather than float.

Harness choice directly affects your kite control. A waist harness from ION or NeilPryde gives you the freedom of movement and core engagement needed for explosive pops. Seat harnesses work too, but they limit your mobility during the takeoff phase. Look for a harness with a mid-range hook position (not too low) so you can engage bar pressure smoothly without excessive arm strain.

For water temperature, choose a 3/2mm or 4/3mm wetsuit from ION or NeilPryde depending on your session location and season. Big air sessions are intense and short—you're burning energy fast—so don't underestimate warmth needs.

Technique Tips

Harness settings and technique are inseparable. Keep your bar pressure consistent and your hands relaxed on the bar. Many jumpers over-grip, which exhausts the arms and kills the smooth kite movement you need. Position your hands shoulder-width apart, and use your core and leg drive rather than arm strength to generate pop.

As you approach your jump, edge hard downwind to load the board and build tension in the lines. Your kite should be sitting at roughly the 12 o'clock position (directly overhead or slightly forward). When you commit to the jump, snap the bar towards your torso with a quick, explosive movement while your legs drive into extension. This timing is everything: the bar movement triggers the kite's lift just as your legs release, amplifying your vertical lift.

Once airborne, steer your kite slightly forward (towards 11 o'clock) to maintain height and control. Keep the bar pulled in during the flight to maximise lift. As you descend, prepare for a smooth landing by extending your legs and positioning your board perpendicular to the wind so you can ride away cleanly.

Safety Checklist

  • Launch zone: Always check that you have clear water downwind and upwind. Avoid jumping near the shore, other riders, or obstacles.
  • Harness integrity: Inspect your harness hook and waist connector before every session. A failing hook under load during a jump can be catastrophic.
  • Line and kite condition: Big air puts extreme load on your lines and kite. Check for fraying, knots, or damage. Replace lines and kites that show wear.
  • Recovery plan: Always have a clear, downwind exit route. If you lose control mid-air, you need space to land safely without collision.
  • Buddy system: Never jump alone. Have a spotter or training partner who can provide a rescue board or assistance if needed.
  • Progressive progression: Build height gradually. Don't attempt massive jumps in untested wind or after a long break from the sport.
  • Helmet and impact protection: Head and spine injuries are real risks in big air. Wear a certified water sports helmet and consider a padded impact vest.

Our Gear Recommendations at Surf Store

We stock Duotone and Cabrinha kites in the 13–15m range—both brands deliver the predictable, responsive feel you need for jumping. Pair your kite with a Nobile, Fanatic, or Cabrinha freestyle board in the 135–150cm range that suits your weight and riding style. For harnesses, we recommend ION or NeilPryde waist harnesses with adjustable hook positions so you can fine-tune your bar pressure and comfort. Complete your setup with a 3/2mm or 4/3mm ION or NeilPryde wetsuit to stay warm during intense sessions. Visit surf-store.com to browse our full range and get personalised advice from our team—we've all jumped big air and can help you dial in the right setup for your conditions and ability level.

title: Windsurf Harness Settings for Strong Wind — Tuning Guide
meta_title: Windsurf Harness Strong Wind Tips 2026 | Surf Store
meta_description: Master windsurf harness settings for strong wind conditions. Expert tips on technique, gear selection & safety. Find it at Surf Store.
excerpt: Learn how to adjust your windsurf harness, technique, and gear selection when strong wind strikes your local spot.
body_html:
Understanding These Conditions

Strong wind in windsurfing typically means 25–35+ knots of steady breeze, where wave height builds noticeably and your sail power increases dramatically. At this level, your harness becomes your most critical piece of equipment—it absorbs enormous force through your back, legs, and core, allowing you to stay connected to the board without exhausting your arms.

These are the conditions where windsurf harness settings separate comfortable, controlled riding from struggling battles with overpowering sails. The margin between exhilaration and danger narrows significantly. Wave action can be choppy or well-formed depending on fetch, water temperature drops, and visibility may reduce. This is when proper harness positioning, hook angle, and pressure distribution become non-negotiable.

Best Gear for These Conditions

In strong wind, your sail size shrinks dramatically. Most riders move to 4.0–5.2m wave sails from brands like Duotone, NeilPryde, or Gaastra—these are designed to remain controllable in gusty, powerful conditions whilst still providing drive. Smaller sails mean faster boom speeds and tighter turning, so your harness must work even harder.

Your board should be a wave board in the 70–85-litre range, around 220–240cm in length. Compact, responsive designs from JP Australia, Fanatic, or Tabou excel here; they manoeuvre instantly and shed excess water. Larger freestyle or freeride boards become unmanageable in these gusts.

Wetsuits thicken up significantly. A 4/3mm or 5/4mm suit from ION or NeilPryde is standard, depending on water temperature. Gloves become advisable to maintain grip and protect against cold spray. Your harness itself should be a waist harness with a low-profile hook and secure waist attachment—look at models from Duotone or Cabrinha that balance chest support with back protection without excessive bulk.

Technique Tips

Hook positioning is paramount in strong wind. Your harness hook should sit lower and slightly further back on the boom than in light air. This shifts pressure away from your shoulders and into your core and legs, where your body is strongest. A hook that sits too high or too far forward will exhaust your arms in minutes.

Pressure distribution matters enormously. In strong windsurf harness settings, lean back slightly more than you would in 15 knots—your upper body weight counterbalances the sail's pull. Your legs should be engaged: feet planted firmly on the footstraps, knees bent, and hips stable. The harness absorbs the sail force; your legs drive the board.

Keep your boom lower than usual. A lower boom reduces weather helm, makes the sail easier to handle, and allows your body weight to distribute more evenly through the harness. Gybe and tack with purpose; hesitation in strong wind causes wipeouts. Commit fully to each manoeuvre.

Stay loose in your upper body. Tension locks up the harness and makes subtle sail adjustments impossible. Your arms should be relaxed guides; the harness and legs do the work. This rhythm takes practice but transforms strong-wind sessions from survival into flow.

Safety Checklist

  • Harness attachment: Check that your waist belt is tight, hook is clean and undamaged, and the carabiner or loop system is secure before every session. A failed harness connection at 30 knots is catastrophic.
  • Boom height: Ensure your boom sits at elbow height or slightly lower. Too-high booms force awkward arm positions and increase shoulder strain and injury risk.
  • Sail size vs. skill: In strong wind, resist the urge to sail too large a sail. An undersized sail is recoverable; an oversized one in a gust leaves you helpless. Err on the side of caution.
  • Buddy system: Never sail strong-wind conditions alone. Ensure at least one other experienced sailor is on the water and within sight.
  • Weather window: Check forecasts and local wind patterns. Strong wind with a rising or unstable pressure system is riskier than established, steady strong breeze.
  • Recovery plan: Know your exit route. Identify a downwind beach or recovery point before you launch. In strong wind, getting back is harder than going out.
  • Core strength: Strong-wind harness work demands a strong core. If your back or abdomen fatigues quickly, shorten sessions and focus on technique over duration.

Our Gear Recommendations at Surf Store

At Surf Store, we specialise in equipping riders for exactly these challenging conditions. For strong wind, we stock Duotone and Cabrinha wave sails in the 4.0–5.2m range, designed to remain stable and responsive in gusts and chop. Pair these with a JP Australia, Fanatic, or Tabou wave board in the 70–85-litre range—all offer the manoeuvrability and durability that strong-wind riding demands.

For harnesses, we carry Duotone and Cabrinha waist harnesses with low-profile hooks and secure attachment systems. These are engineered for the weight distribution and pressure management that strong conditions require. Match your harness with ION or NeilPryde 4/3mm and 5/4mm wetsuits to stay warm and responsive, plus gloves from these same brands to maintain grip and dexterity.

Visit us at Surf Store to talk through your strong-wind setup with our team. We have on-water experience across all these brands and conditions, and we'll help you build a rig that keeps you safe, comfortable, and in control when the wind howls. We ship across Europe in EUR, and every purchase comes with expert guidance.

title: Stuck in a Kitesurfing Rut? — How to Progress Faster
meta_title: Kitesurfing Progress Tips: Improve Faster in 2026
meta_description: Master kitesurfing faster with expert tips on technique, equipment, wind conditions, and training. Break through plateaus at Surf Store.
excerpt: Progress faster in kitesurfing by combining consistent practice in variable wind, deliberate technique work, proper equipment progression, and learning from ...
body_html:
The fastest way to progress in kitesurfing is to combine focused, deliberate practice with the right equipment and professional guidance. Most riders waste time repeating the same mistakes instead of isolating specific skills, choosing boards and kites that don't match their level, or neglecting the mental side of progression. Breaking through plateaus means being intentional about what you practise each session.

The Full Answer

Kitesurfing progress isn't linear, and many riders hit a wall around intermediate level because they switch from simply enjoying the water to expecting measurable improvement. To progress faster, you need to understand that kitesurfing progress tips fall into three overlapping areas: technical skill, mental discipline, and equipment matching.

Technical progression means breaking tricks and manoeuvres into micro-skills. Rather than attempting a full air or a transition, you isolate the pop, the rotation, the landing. You film yourself, study what went wrong, and repeat the drill until it's automatic. Professional riders spend 70% of their time on fundamentals—edge control, pop consistency, and body positioning—not attempting advanced tricks.

Equipment is often the hidden blocker. Riders stay on beginner or all-rounder boards too long, then jump to a board that's too small or stiff. Similarly, many cling to a single kite size instead of learning to feel control across different wind ranges. The right kit for your current level accelerates learning dramatically.

Finally, improve faster by building a community. Riding with others at your level, getting feedback from instructors, and competing or comparing yourself to peers creates accountability and fresh perspective. Solo sessions are great for consolidation, but stagnation often follows isolation.

Practical Guide

1. Film every session. Even a mobile phone captures enough to spot flaws. Most riders can't feel the difference between a clean pop and a sloppy one—they need to see it. Review footage within 24 hours while the session is fresh.

2. Isolate one skill per session. Don't practise everything. On Monday, focus purely on your edge control and pressure transitions. On Wednesday, drill your pop timing. On Friday, work on landing consistency. This targeted approach means measurable progress each week.

3. Get professional tuition once quarterly. A qualified instructor catches ingrained faults that peers miss. Even one session can unblock months of frustration. Our expert staff at Surf Store regularly guide progression riders.

4. Choose boards for your level. If you're improving tricks and planning to shift focus, you need a freestyle or trick-specific board in the appropriate volume for your weight and wind conditions. An undersized or inappropriate board will frustrate, not accelerate, progress.

5. Manage kite size and control. Master 1–2 kite sizes thoroughly before expanding your range. Jumping between 5 different sizes means you never develop true feel. Learn the power window and sweet spot of each kite intimately.

6. Keep a progression journal. Note date, location, wind, tricks attempted, successes, and what felt wrong. Over weeks, patterns emerge. You'll see which conditions suit your learning and which drills deliver results fastest.

Common Mistakes

  • Vague practice. Riding for 4 hours without a plan delivers minimal progression. Focused 90-minute sessions beat lazy full-day sessions.
  • Chasing advanced tricks too soon. Attempting backroll loops before your pop, upwind, and edge control are rock-solid wastes energy and builds bad habits.
  • Sticking with beginner gear. Outgrowing your board or kite and not upgrading means you're fighting your kit, not progressing. Progression requires matching equipment to your emerging skills.
  • Neglecting light-wind and strong-wind sessions. Most riders only go out in ideal conditions. Practising in marginal wind teaches control and adaptability, speeding overall progress.
  • Ignoring body mechanics. Poor posture, weak core, and rigid arms kill progression. Off-water training—gym work, yoga, balance drills—matters as much as water time.

Surf Store Recommendation

To progress faster in kitesurfing, match your gear to your ambitions. If you're moving toward freestyle and tricks, we stock freestyle boards from Duotone, Cabrinha, and Nobile in the 135–155 cm range—sizes that favour manoeuvres without sacrificing pop. For your kite, Duotone and Cabrinha offer responsive freestyle and wave kites that reward precise bar input and edge control.

Our expert team regularly advises riders breaking through intermediate plateaus. We'll help you find the board volume and kite size that match your weight, riding style, and local wind window. Visit surf-store.com to browse our full range and contact our staff—we ride these conditions ourselves and understand what accelerates progression.

title: How to Progress Faster in Windsurfing — Breaking Through the Plateau
meta_title: Windsurfing Progress Tips: How to Improve & Break Through...
meta_description: Learn proven windsurfing progress tips to break plateaus and improve faster. Expert guidance on technique, equipment, and training. Find it at Surf Store.
excerpt: Windsurfing progress accelerates when you focus on technique consistency, match your equipment to current conditions, and train with structured feedback from...
body_html:
Faster windsurfing progress comes from focused skill work, the right equipment, consistent practice, and coaching feedback—not just time on the water. Most sailors stall because they practise the same habits repetitively rather than targeting weak points. With intentional training, the right board and sail, and honest self-assessment, you can accelerate your windsurfing progress and break through performance plateaus.

The Full Answer

Windsurfing progress isn't linear, and many sailors hit a wall after their first season. This plateau often happens because beginners learn the fundamentals in isolation—getting up, steering, gybing—without linking them into fluid technique. Once you can stay upwind and complete a gybe, your brain assumes the job is done. It isn't.

Real windsurfing progress happens when you:

  • Diagnose your bottleneck. Are you weak in light wind? Struggling with foot placement in waves? Losing speed in gybes? Pinpoint one weakness each session.
  • Match your equipment to your goals. A beginner needs stability; an intermediate sailor chasing speed needs a narrow board with responsive handling. Mismatched kit kills motivation.
  • Get external feedback. Your body doesn't always know what it's doing wrong. A coach or experienced friend spots angles, weight distribution, and timing issues you'll never see yourself.
  • Practise deliberately. Sail in a specific range of conditions, focus on one manoeuvre per session, and repeat until it's automatic.
  • Track small wins. Progress in windsurfing often feels invisible. Note when you first hold a reach without dropping the boom, complete a smooth roll gybe, or feel genuinely comfortable in 15-knot wind.

Practical Guide

For Light-Wind Improvement (under 10 knots): Light air is where technique shines because you can't muscle the board. Sail a wider board (around 90–110 litres) with a large sail. Focus on harness lines, boom height, and weight distribution. Practise small, efficient movements rather than aggressive pumping. This builds muscle memory that transfers to stronger wind.

For Speed and Gybing: Book a 2–3 hour group lesson with an instructor who focuses on your weak points. Most sailors improve 50% faster with live feedback. Practise gybes in 12–15 knot wind on a narrow board (70–90 litres) that forces good technique. Your weight placement and boom handling matter more than speed here.

For Wave Sailing: Move to shorter, wider boards (80–95 litres) that forgive mistakes. Sail in choppy, waist-high conditions where you're constantly re-balancing. Wave sailing builds reactive balance faster than flat-water freestyle.

Consistency matters more than heroic sessions. Two hours every other day beats one eight-hour marathon weekend. Your nervous system needs time to consolidate skills.

Common Mistakes

Using equipment that's too small or narrow. Many intermediate sailors jump to high-performance boards too soon. If you're wrestling the kit, you're not improving—you're fighting. Stay on a slightly forgiving board and focus on technique.

Ignoring video feedback. Record yourself, or ask a friend to film a session. Watching yourself gybe, tack, or jibe reveals posture and timing errors that feel invisible in the moment. This is free windsurfing progress coaching.

Practising only in your comfort zone. Progression requires small stress. If you only sail in 15 knots, you'll never master 8-knot trim or 20-knot control. Sail in slightly variable conditions.

Skipping the basics. Many sailors blame their board for poor speed, but the real issue is boom height, harness grip, or foot pressure. Master the fundamentals first.

Surf Store Recommendation

To support faster windsurfing progress, you'll need the right platform. We stock freeride and freestyle boards from JP Australia, Fanatic, and Tabou in sizes 70–110 litres—boards designed for skill development without fighting. Pair these with quality sails from Duotone, NeilPryde, or Gaastra in ranges suited to your wind zone. If you're serious about breaking your plateau, invest in a narrower board (75–85 litres) for manoeuvre practice and a wider freeride option (95–110 litres) for consistency sessions. Our team has tested every board in real conditions across European waters—contact us to match your size, weight, and skill level with the kit that'll actually help you progress. title: First Time Kitesurfing in Open Water — What to Expect
meta_title: Kitesurfing First Time Ocean Tips 2026 | Surf Store
meta_description: Essential kitesurfing first time ocean tips: safety checks, conditions, gear setup. Start confidently. Shop at Surf Store.
excerpt: Before your first kitesurfing session in open water, ensure you have proper training, check weather and tides, inspect your gear thoroughly, and never go alone.
body_html:
Kitesurfing first time in open water demands respect for wind, water conditions, and your own limits. The ocean is unpredictable, so you need solid fundamentals, proper equipment tuned to your skill level, and ideally a spotter or instructor nearby. Most importantly: never assume you're ready until you've practised in protected spots and truly understand how your kite responds to wind shifts.

The Full Answer

Transitioning from a lagoon or bay to open water is a milestone moment in kitesurfing, but it's not just about doing more of what you've already learned. Open water introduces swell, stronger currents, wind gusts that change without warning, and fewer escape routes if things go wrong. Your first time in the ocean should not be your first time on a board with a kite.

Before you head out, you need genuine comfort with:

  • Wind reading: Understanding how wind strength changes across the water, spotting gusts and lulls, and knowing your kite's sweet spot in different conditions.
  • Safety systems: Your quick-release, your harness settings, and how to self-rescue without panic.
  • Board control: Solid edge awareness, ability to stop and hold position, and confidence in choppy water.
  • Fitness and endurance: Open water sessions are longer and more physically demanding than protected-water practice.

Many kitesurfers skip protected-water training and rush into ocean sessions, thinking they're ready after a few lessons. This is where injuries and close calls happen. The ocean doesn't care about your confidence—it tests your actual skill.

Practical Guide

Choose the right conditions for your first session. Look for a day with steady, moderate wind (12–16 knots), small swell, and a sandy beach with no rocks, piers, or obstacles downwind. Avoid tidal changes, offshore wind, or crowded breaks where you'll feel pressured to perform.

Scout the spot beforehand. Visit the beach on a calm day to understand currents, understand exit points, and identify where other water users operate. Talk to locals if they're there—they know hazards you won't see from the shore.

Go with a partner or instructor. Even if you're confident, having someone on the beach watching and ready to call you in is invaluable. An instructor who knows that specific break is even better—they'll spot wind patterns and currents you'd miss alone.

Check your gear thoroughly. Before you launch, inspect your kite for line damage, your board for pressure damage, your harness buckles, and your quick-release mechanism. A few minutes on shore beats 20 minutes panicking in the water.

Start at the water's edge. Fly your kite standing up for 2–3 minutes before you strap in. Feel how it responds to wind shifts. If it feels twitchy or unstable, conditions may be stronger than forecast, or your kite pressure is wrong—go back onshore and adjust.

Exit into the wind, not downwind. When you're done or tired, always head upwind to exit. Drifting downwind and trying to walk back is exhausting and dangerous.

Common Mistakes

Underestimating current and drift. Beginners often think they can hold position easily, then discover they're being swept 200 metres along the coast without realising it. Stay aware of where the beach is and how far you've drifted.

Using a board that's too small. In choppy open water, a smaller board is harder to control and easier to lose. Use a board with enough volume to stay buoyant and stable even when you're tired.

Ignoring fatigue. Your first open-water session will tire you faster than you expect. As soon as you feel your edge control slipping or your balance feeling off, get back to shore. Pride comes before a long swim.

Flying an unfamiliar kite. If you've always trained on a Duotone or Cabrinha, don't switch to a different brand the day before your ocean session. Stick with what you know until you're confident in new conditions.

Launching in light wind hoping it picks up. It rarely does, and you'll waste energy and frustration trying to stay airborne. If you can't fly your kite immediately at the shore, the wind isn't there yet.

Surf Store Recommendation

For your first open-water sessions, choose a stable wave or freestyle board from brands like Nobile, Fanatic, or JP Australia—something with enough volume for your weight that forgives mistakes. Pair it with a reliable all-round kite from Duotone, Cabrinha, or NeilPryde in the size you've trained on. Don't experiment with new gear in new conditions.

If you're unsure about your readiness, we recommend one or two sessions with a qualified instructor at the location where you want to progress. It's money well spent. Visit surf-store.com to browse our complete range of kitesurfing boards, kites, and harnesses, or contact our team if you want advice on gear suited to your local conditions and skill level.

title: How to Kitesurf Upwind Faster — Technique & Tips
meta_title: Kitesurf Upwind Faster: Technique Tips 2026
meta_description: Master how to kitesurf upwind faster with proven technique tips. Learn efficient body position, kite control & more. Shop gear at Surf Store.
excerpt: Efficient upwind kitesurfing depends on precise kite angles, board positioning, and body weight distribution to maximise forward progress against the wind.
body_html:
Efficient upwind kitesurfing boils down to three core elements: maintaining the correct body position over your board, managing your kite's power delivery, and angling your board to minimise drift. Most beginners lose ground to windward because they fight the wind rather than work with it—the trick is using your kite to generate forward speed while your board's edge holds your line.

The Full Answer

Kitesurfing upwind is fundamentally different from downwind riding. When you're heading into the wind, your kite does most of the work pushing you forward, but your board's edge and your body weight determine whether you actually move upwind or simply get blown sideways. The angle at which you approach the wind, combined with how aggressively you edge your board, directly affects how much ground you gain.

Your kite position matters enormously. A kite flying at 45 degrees generates maximum forward power, whereas a kite that's too high (closer to 12 o'clock) produces mostly lift—useful for tricks, not for upwind progress. Your body angle also changes the game: leaning back slightly transfers power to your edge, while hunching forward robs you of leverage and control. Finally, your board itself must bite into the water through edge pressure; a loose, flat board will simply skitter downwind regardless of your technique.

Practical Guide

Step 1: Set Your Kite Angle
Position your kite at approximately 45 degrees—imagine it's between one and two o'clock if you're riding on your right hand. This angle balances forward power with just enough lift to keep you stable. If your kite drifts too high, you'll feel more pull and less forward drive; too low, and it loses power entirely.

Step 2: Commit to Your Edge
The single biggest mistake is riding flat-footed. Press your heels (or toes, depending on which way you're riding) hard into the board. This creates an edge that resists the sideways pressure of the wind and lets your board grip the water. Your weight distribution should favour the downwind edge—this is what actually pushes you upwind.

Step 3: Keep Your Body Aligned
Your shoulders should face slightly upwind of your board's direction. Lean back from the waist, using the kite's power to support your weight. This posture allows your legs to drive the edge without your upper body collapsing forward. Many riders lean too far forward and lose all mechanical advantage.

Step 4: Small, Frequent Kite Movements
Rather than yanking your kite, make small adjustments. Gentle dives (moving your kite downward) increase power when you need a burst to punch through choppy water or angle steeper upwind. Soft movements preserve your balance and let you read the wind's changes.

Step 5: Choose the Right Board
A directional board with a pronounced edge will hold your line far better than a symmetrical twin-tip. Similarly, smaller boards require more edge pressure but reward you with better upwind angle. Larger boards forgive sloppy technique but sacrifice upwind performance.

Common Mistakes

  • Riding too flat: Pressing neither edge nor heel creates zero grip. Water flows under your board, and wind pushes you sideways.
  • Kite too high: A kite at 12 o'clock gives you lift but minimal forward drive. You'll feel tugged upward, not propelled forward.
  • Hunching forward: This kills leverage and makes your legs weak. Your kite ends up working against your body's weak posture.
  • Over-correcting with the bar: Big, jerky inputs destabilise you. Smooth, small movements work better.
  • Wrong board choice: Trying to go upwind on a large freestyle board is like steering a barge. Directional boards and smaller twin-tips excel at upwind.

Surf Store Recommendation

To dial in your upwind game, you'll benefit from a board designed for riding in all directions. We stock directional and wave boards from Fanatic and JP Australia, as well as responsive freestyle and all-round boards from Duotone, Cabrinha, and Nobile. Each brand offers models across the size range, and our team can help you pick one suited to your weight, wind conditions, and skill level. Better edge hold, better upwind performance.

On the kite side, every major brand—Duotone, Cabrinha, NeilPryde, and Gaastra—designs their wings to perform across the wind window. A kite with good bar feedback lets you feel exactly where that 45-degree power zone is, making upwind technique easier to learn and refine.

Visit surf-store.com to browse our range of boards and kites, or reach out to our team with your weight, local wind window, and riding goals. We'll point you toward kit that turns your upwind ambitions into real progress on the water.

title: How to Jump Kitesurfing — First Jump Guide for Beginners
meta_title: How to Jump Kitesurfing First Time | 2026 Guide
meta_description: Learn how to jump kitesurfing safely as a beginner. Master technique, timing, and kite control. Find expert gear at Surf Store.
excerpt: Your first kitesurfing jump comes from combining proper kite control, board positioning, and explosive leg power at the right moment.
body_html:
Learning to jump for the first time kitesurfing is one of the most exciting milestones in your progression. The secret isn't raw power or bravery—it's understanding where to place your kite, how to build and release edge tension at the right moment, and practising in forgiving conditions with equipment that suits your level.

The Full Answer

Your first kitesurfing jump happens when you combine three core elements: a powered kite (usually at 45 degrees off the wind), aggressive heel edge pressure on your board, and a sharp, explosive pop as you redirect the kite upward. The kite lift pulls you skyward whilst your board's edge release frees your feet from the water. Most beginners can achieve their first jump after 40–60 hours of solid practise, provided they've nailed carving, edge control, and body awareness.

The reason many struggle is they overthink it. You're not trying to jump athletically like a wakeboarder—you're using the kite's power. When you pop your edge and move the kite toward the zenith, the combined lift launches you. Timing is everything.

Practical Guide

Step 1: Master Your Edge
Before attempting a jump, spend weeks just riding on your heel edge with tension and control. Carve hard. Feel how pressure on your heels keeps the board locked in. This foundation prevents you from slipping and gives you a solid platform to pop from.

Step 2: Find the Right Spot
Choose a shallow, spacious bay with consistent, moderate wind (12–16 knots for most riders). Avoid crowds and shallow obstacles. You want room to land without risk and flat or slightly choppy water—glass-smooth water can be harder because there's less friction to slow your landing.

Step 3: Approach Your Jump
Ride upwind on your heel edge, building speed and tension. Keep your kite at around 45 degrees off the wind. Look ahead, not down. Stay relaxed but focused.

Step 4: The Pop and Redirect
As you're riding, snap the bar toward you explosively (pop your heel edge hard into the water) whilst steering your kite quickly toward 12 o'clock. This double action—edge pop plus kite redirect—creates lift. Your legs will naturally extend upward as the kite climbs. Keep your weight centred and your core engaged.

Step 5: The Flight
Once airborne, relax slightly and stay tall. Bring your knees up gently toward your chest—this gives you body awareness and preparation for landing. Keep the kite moving smoothly upwind to maintain height.

Step 6: Land Cleanly
Lower the kite slightly as you descend, flex your knees on impact, and immediately edge away from the kite to regain control. Your first landing won't be pretty, and that's fine.

Common Mistakes

  • Kite too low: Many beginners keep their kite below 45 degrees. It won't generate enough lift. Move it higher, toward 10 or 11 o'clock, for genuine pop.
  • Poor edge pressure: If you haven't built solid edge control, your pop will be weak and inconsistent. Invest time in carving and controlled riding first.
  • Steering the kite too slowly: The kite redirect must be sharp and immediate. A sluggish steering input won't create the sudden lift you need.
  • Looking down: Beginners often stare at the water. This collapses your posture. Keep your head up and your eyes on the horizon.
  • Wrong wind conditions: Jumping in very light wind (under 10 knots) or very strong wind (over 18 knots) makes it harder to control the mechanics. Aim for consistent, moderate winds.

Surf Store Recommendation

To land your first jump safely, you'll want a board and kite that reward good technique without punishing mistakes. We stock freestyle and wave boards from Duotone, Cabrinha, and Nobile in the 135–150cm range—sizes that give excellent pop and response for beginners and intermediate riders. Pair these with a freestyle-focused kite from Duotone, Cabrinha, or NeilPryde in the 14–17m range, depending on your weight and local wind. Our team at Surf Store has years of on-water experience and can recommend exactly what suits your size, weight, and learning pace. Visit us online or get in touch—we're here to help you nail those first jumps.

title: How to Tack in Kitesurfing — Transition Technique Guide
meta_title: How to Tack in Kitesurfing 2026 | Transition Technique Guide
meta_description: Master the kitesurfing tack technique with our step-by-step guide. Learn transitions, avoid common mistakes, and ride better. Shop quality kites at Surf...
excerpt: A tack in kitesurfing is a 180° board rotation where you change direction by turning into the wind and switching feet and kite position.
body_html:
A tack in kitesurfing is a transition where you change direction by turning upwind through the wind window. You switch your feet to the opposite end of the board, move your kite across the zenith (top of the wind window), and reposition your hands on the bar to face the new direction. It's one of the most useful transitions for navigating spots, regaining upwind position, and riding efficiently in real conditions.

The Full Answer

The how to transition tack kitesurfing technique starts with understanding why you'd use it. Unlike a jibe—where you turn downwind—a tack takes you upwind. This is crucial for staying in the best riding zone, avoiding obstacles, or heading back to your starting point. The transition demands coordination between your board control, body weight, and kite steering.

When you tack, your kite travels through the weakest part of the wind window (near the zenith). This means you lose power temporarily, so your board's momentum carries you through the turn. Your goal is to keep moving forward, smoothly rotate your body and board, and then re-engage the kite on the other side to maintain speed.

The transition typically takes 3–5 seconds for intermediate riders, though beginners may take longer. Timing is everything: you need enough speed to float through the turn without power, but enough kite control to position yourself correctly on the other side.

Practical Guide

Step 1: Build Speed and Prepare
Ride upwind on a reach (at an angle to the wind) to build momentum. Keep your kite at 11 or 1 o'clock (whichever side you're on), and stay relaxed with weight on your heels.

Step 2: Initiate the Turn
Steer your board upwind by pushing your back foot down and leaning back slightly. As your nose turns toward the wind, begin moving your kite toward the zenith (12 o'clock). Move slowly and deliberately—sudden movements lose control.

Step 3: Unweight the Board
As you approach the wind, shift your weight forward over the centre of the board. This helps it pivot more easily and reduces friction as you transition.

Step 4: Switch Your Feet
As the board rotates, walk your feet to the opposite end. Your rear foot becomes your new front foot, and vice versa. Keep your knees bent for balance.

Step 5: Reposition Your Kite
Once your board points to the other side of the wind window, steer your kite to the new 11 or 1 o'clock position. This is where you'll catch the wind again.

Step 6: Re-engage Power
As your kite picks up wind on the new side, sink your weight back onto your back foot to edge the board and control the power. You should feel pressure return to the bar—that's your cue you've completed the transition.

Common Mistakes

  • Moving the kite too fast through the zenith: This causes you to stall or lose the board entirely. Keep kite movements smooth and gradual.
  • Failing to build enough speed: Without momentum, you'll lose the board and sink. Always approach a tack at riding speed.
  • Not switching feet properly: Hesitating during the foot swap causes you to fall off-balance. Commit to the movement decisively.
  • Positioning the kite too low after the transition: Your kite should return to a working angle (around 45° from zenith), not drop to the edge of the window.
  • Over-steering the board: Turning too much past the wind means you'll end up pointing downwind and waste your tack attempt.

Surf Store Recommendation

Tacking is easier on a board with good float and forgiveness. We stock all-round and freeride boards from Cabrinha, Duotone, JP Australia, and Nobile in sizes suited to all weights and skill levels. A wider, more buoyant board (120–150cm, depending on your weight) gives you the margin for error beginners need when learning transitions. Our team can match you with the right size and feel—visit surf-store.com to browse our range or contact our expert staff for personalised advice.

title: How to Gybe in Kitesurfing — Step-by-Step Technique Guide
meta_title: How to Gybe in Kitesurfing: Technique & Steps 2026
meta_description: Learn how to gybe in kitesurfing with our step-by-step guide. Master this essential technique safely. Shop kite gear at Surf Store.
excerpt: Gybing in kitesurfing is a controlled 180° turn where you redirect your kite and board to change direction, done by flying the kite overhead and shifting you...
body_html:
A gybe (also spelled jibe) in kitesurfing is a controlled 180° directional change where you transition your weight, steer your board, and reposition your kite to head in the opposite direction. It's one of the first tricks riders learn after getting comfortable with basic riding, and mastering it transforms you from a beginner to an intermediate kitesurfer.

The Full Answer

The how to gybe in kitesurfing technique involves four simultaneous actions: maintaining kite control, shifting your stance, rotating your board through the wind, and preparing for the opposite tack. Unlike sailing, where the boom swings across, kitesurfing gybes are rider-initiated and more dynamic.

The gybe differs from a tack (turning into the wind) because you're turning away from the wind, keeping power in the kite throughout. Your kite stays active on one side of the window, dives slightly to generate forward momentum, and then you pivot your board beneath you while keeping both feet locked in the straps. When executed cleanly, you'll feel your board pivot smoothly as the kite provides drive.

Why learn this? Gybes let you extend your riding range, navigate narrow channels, position yourself for better wind, and eventually link tricks together. Every serious kitesurfer needs reliable gybes in their toolkit.

Practical Guide

Step 1: Choose your setup
Find a flat water area with steady wind (ideally 12–16 knots for beginners). You need enough space to complete the turn without obstacles. Check that both your board and kite are in good condition.

Step 2: Build speed and edge control
Ride across the wind at comfortable speed, keeping your board edged slightly upwind. Feel the pressure in both feet and the balance point of your bar. Relax your shoulders—tension kills smooth turns.

Step 3: Initiate the turn
Look over your shoulder in the direction you want to turn. Dive your kite toward the edge of the window (if turning left, dive the kite left and slightly forward). This generates extra power and tips your board's nose.

Step 4: Rotate your hips and shoulders
As the kite dives, rotate your hips hard in the direction of the turn. Keep your weight forward on the balls of your feet. Your shoulders follow your hips—this rotation drives the board around.

Step 5: Pop the board through the turn
As the board pivots, push down on your back foot momentarily, then shift weight to the new front foot as you complete the 180°. The board should slide slightly, not spin in place.

Step 6: Redirect the kite
As you finish the rotation, steer the kite back toward the middle of the window on the new side. This re-powers the kite and generates forward drive on your new heading.

Step 7: Exit and extend
Ride away smoothly, keeping your weight centred. You should exit the gybe with speed and control, ready to continue or link another manoeuvre.

Common Mistakes

  • Diving the kite too hard or too far: Over-diving stalls the kite or loses power. Dive smoothly and keep the kite moving through the window.
  • Not rotating your hips: Arm rotation alone won't turn the board. Your core drives the manoeuvre; use your whole body.
  • Leaning back: Hanging on the bar pulls you away from the pivot point. Stay forward and low during the turn.
  • Losing board control mid-turn: Rushing the foot switch causes the board to slip unpredictably. Slow, deliberate weight transfer wins.
  • Forgetting to re-power the kite: If you leave the kite parked on one side, you'll stall and lose momentum. Steer it actively back toward the middle.

Surf Store Recommendation

Learning to gybe requires the right equipment. We recommend freestyle boards from Duotone, Cabrinha, and Nobile in the 135–150cm range—they're forgiving, pivot easily, and give you feedback as you progress. Pair them with a mid-sized directional or hybrid kite from Duotone, Cabrinha, or NeilPryde (13–17m) to build confidence without fighting the conditions.

We stock gear suitable for every level, and our team has real on-water experience. If you're unsure which board or kite suits your style and progression, contact Surf Store for expert guidance. We ship across Europe and are happy to advise on technique and equipment together.

title: How to Waterstart Windsurfing — Beginner Step-by-Step Guide
meta_title: How to Waterstart Windsurfing 2026 | Beginner Guide
meta_description: Master the waterstart in windsurfing with our step-by-step beginner guide. Learn technique, avoid common mistakes, and progress faster. Shop at Surf Store.
excerpt: A waterstart in windsurfing is when you launch into the wind from deep water by using the sail's power to lift you and the board onto the plane without needi...
body_html:
A waterstart is a technique where you launch yourself onto the board from deep water by using the sail's power and body positioning to generate upward force. Unlike a beach start, you're already in the water, which means you need to generate enough leverage with the sail to lift yourself and the board towards the surface. It's one of the most useful skills in windsurfing, and once you've cracked it, you'll dramatically expand the conditions where you can keep sailing.

The Full Answer

The waterstart in windsurfing is fundamentally about using the sail as a lifting force whilst your body and legs act as an anchor point on the board. When conditions are too choppy or you've fallen away from shore, a waterstart lets you get going again without wading back to land. The technique relies on three key elements: board flotation, sail control, and body positioning.

Most windsurfing waterstarts happen when you're floating in deep water with the board upside down or alongside you. You'll position yourself so that the sail is powered but controlled, then use the rising force from the wind to pull you and the board upright and into sailing position. The beauty of a good waterstart is that it works across a wide range of wind strengths—light wind waterstarts require perfect technique, whilst strong wind ones are more forgiving because the sail generates more power naturally.

Practical Guide

Step 1: Position Yourself and the Board

Float on your back or side with the board next to you in the water. Get the sail out of the water and held at an angle so it catches wind but isn't pulling you over yet. The boom should be roughly horizontal, and you should be able to feel the sail wanting to lift.

Step 2: Control Your Entry Point

Position yourself at the side of the board, roughly at the mast track or slightly behind it. This is crucial—too far forward and you'll flip; too far back and the nose will dive. Your feet should be ready to plant on the board near the centre line.

Step 3: Use Your Arms and Core

As the sail begins to lift you, keep the boom grip firm but allow your arms to straighten. Let the sail pull you upward rather than fighting it. Your core muscles should be engaged to help rotate your body towards the board.

Step 4: Plant Your Feet

As you rise, aim to get one foot (usually your back foot first) onto the board near the centre. This gives you a pivot point. The sail's power will continue lifting as you do this, and your other foot should follow quickly.

Step 5: Come Into Sailing Position

Once both feet are on the board and it's beginning to right itself, shift your weight to a normal sailing stance. Sheet in the sail gradually and steer away from the wind to maintain control. You're now sailing—congratulations.

Common Mistakes

Positioning too far forward: This is the number one beginner error. If you climb too far up the board, the nose submerges and you go over the top. Stay mid-board or slightly back.

Not sheeting in enough: You need the sail to be powered to generate lift. Beginners often hold the sail too far out, reducing power and making the waterstart impossible.

Fighting the sail's pull: Let the sail work for you. Resist the urge to pull yourself up manually—the sail should do most of the work.

Trying in too little wind: Waterstarts are much harder in light winds because the sail generates less power. Build confidence in moderate winds first, then progress to lighter conditions as your technique improves.

We stock wave and freeride boards from Duotone, JP Australia, and Fanatic in sizes that offer excellent flotation and forgiveness for beginners working on this skill. Pair them with sails from Duotone or NeilPryde—their freewave and wave ranges are responsive and predictable, which helps you focus on technique rather than fighting the gear. We also stock wetsuits from ION and Gaastra to keep you comfortable during those repetitive practice sessions in the water.
